boardgame: Das kleine Bankett und andere Abenteuer
description:
The playing area for Das kleine Bankett und andere Abenteuer ("The Little Banquet and Other Adventures") in most of the game's scenarios is a tad unusual: a circle of chairs, with players starting each round by standing behind a chair. The players represent kids from different countries, specifically brothers and sisters who are accompanied by a pet, and they want to solve puzzles, quests and tasks while meeting and playing with one another. More specifically, some scenarios involve cooperative tasks, while another might have you compete against everyone in a race. Each scenario has been designed to be replayable numerous times.

Once a scenario begins, a golden "ball" will be put into play from the starting point, moving either clockwise or anticlockwise. As soon as the golden ball reaches the first standing player, this player is in charge and can choose to act. Every player has a character card, which depicts his available moves on its backside. Once the player chooses an action, it will likely require other players to move, so the succession of players will change in the circle of chairs.

After his action, the player sits and hands the golden ball to the next player adjacent to him. The next standing player who receives the golden ball takes his turn, and play continues until all players are seated. At this point you check whether you've met any of the winning conditions of the scenario, such as placing members of your faction near goals or hidden treasures, or handing someone a gift. If you manage to exchange places at the right time with your partners, you can achieve different goals; since each character has different abilities to move others around or place themselves near a goal, players often have multiple solutions on their fingertips – assuming they can find them in time.

Each character also has a loyal pet shown on its card. The pets can be one of two classes – Growler or Kuddler – and each class has its own specific move. If needed, a pet's owner can decide to use the action of his pet instead of his own.

Integrates with


    The Last Banquet

The Amazing Spider-Man Labyrinth uses the same basic game play as Ravensburger's decades-old title The aMAZEing Labyrinth. Players try to move through a labyrinthine game board in order to reach a desired symbol shown on a game board tile that matches a hidden card held by this player. The game board consists of a number of fixed tiles as well as rows and columns in which tiles can slide back and forth; these tiles show tunnels – T-shaped, straight, and otherwise – and a player can move his token only along the path as it exists on his turn.

At the start of his turn, however, he takes the one tile on the side of the game board and pushes it into a row or column of his choice, shifting the tunnels and pushing out one tile that the next player will use. This player now moves his pawn, and if he reaches the desired symbol, he reveals the card, then looks at the next card in his stack to see what he wants to reach next turn. Once a player has revealed all of his cards, he needs to return to his starting corner. The first player to do this wins!

To this basic game, The Amazing Spider-Man Labyrinth adds one twist, with the Lizard having a presence on the game board in a variant included with the base game.

Reimplements


    Labyrinth

A short childrens/party game of speed and memory. Each player is dealt six cards and puts them down next to each other in memorized order. Then each player turns over one card, looks at the other players cards, and the first to turn over a second card identical to one already turned over wins the duel. This person loses the card. The game is over when one person has no cards left.

Puzzle Playing Cards® brings puzzle building and card games together. With a variety of different learning styles, you and others work competitively to collect and complete 13 animal puzzles.

Players will collect 4 cards and build hands of each Animal Puzzle. With 13 Puzzles in the game, each puzzle is broken into 4 corner pieces. Each card will have Animal facts, unique colors, and partial images to help guide you to completing your puzzle.

During the game players will pick up a random card from the deck or previously discarded face up card. At the end of a turn you will always discard down to 4 cards at the end of a turn.

Players will all aim to collect all four parts to a puzzle. The first player to do so announces this and all players will collect points, depending on the completion of their targeted puzzle.

The game is designed with many different learning styles in mind and it is encouraged that younger players read their completed puzzle facts out.

The aim of the game is to place company cards as quickly as possible on the board. The board is a map of Brussels. Each square on the map has a different value. The square where to build the companies is chosen by moving two counters by two dice. One dice result is moved vertical the other one horizontal. Whenever the two counters match the same square, one company card can be placed.

A soon as one player has completed his set of companies on the board the game is over and each player adds up the value of his sites.

Dark Village is a role-playing game where simple Citizens are terrorized by Werewolves, Witches, or Demons each night.  With 
suspicions running high, the Citizens choose a person to lynch each day.

Objective
Good and Evil are at odds.  The Citizens must attempt to rid the village of all the evil players by lynching who they suspect to be causing the nighttime murders. If the village is successful 
in lynching all the Werewolves, Witches or Demons during the day, the Citizens win!  If the Evil players succeed in killing all the Citizens, they are victorious!

Components
Dark Village is played with a deck of cards that includes 10 Evil roles and 26 Citizen roles.
          
Setup
A game of Dark Village is easy to get started. Just gather a group of people and have them sit in a circle.  You can be just about anywhere…on chairs, on the ground, around a table, or on the couches in the family room.  A person who is familiar with the game is chosen as the Narrator.  Cards to be used are 
chosen with there being around one Evil player for every 3 Citizens.  The Narrator deals each player a card that becomes their secret role for the game.  A basic game begins with a Narrator, 2 Evil characters and 6 Citizens.

Roles
 Narrator
 Citizens (Good village folk)
 Werewolves, Witches, and Demons (Evil villagers who win by killing off and outnumbering Citizens)
 Seer/Detective (Citizen-sided, can inspect for Evil members)
 Doctor (Citizen-sided, can choose a player to save at night)

Nightfall…
The Narrator begins the game by having everyone close their eyes and be silent.  He controls all the actions in the form of a story. (“Nighttime has fallen and all the village has 
gone to sleep…”) This begins the nighttime phase of the game. Each night, the Narrator has characters awake and perform their actions. He instructs the Werewolves to “awake” (open their eyes) and see their fellow members.  They kill off one of the Citizens by silently gesturing who they choose.  They must all be in agreement. The Narrator asks one Werewolf to gesture his 
battle sign and makes note of it.  The narrator then has the Werewolves go back to “sleep” (close their eyes). The Narrator, then, instructs the Seer to “awake”. The Seer points at a player he suspects of being Evil. The Narrator nods if the Seer is correct.  The Narrator has the Seer go back to “sleep”. The Narrator, next, instructs the Doctor to awake and select a person to save.  The doctor silently points at a player goes back to “sleep”. Last, the Narrator instructs all Citizens to gesture their battle sign and makes note of the sign of the player who was attacked.

Daybreak…
The Narrator has everyone “wake up” to begin the daytime phase.  
Unless the Doctor has saved the player the Werewolves attacked or the player has defeated the Werewolves with his battle sign, a murder is announced to the group.  The Narrator tells a story of that character’s death.  The dead player may no longer participate in the game in any way.  All the players may now discuss who they suspect as one of the killers.  After enough time has passed, the Narrator calls for a vote on who the village will lynch.  The majority vote causes that player to be hung and killed at dusk.

The Second Night
The Narrator continues the night and day phases until, all the Citizens or all the Evil players have been killed.

Winning
When either all Good or all Evil is left, the game is over and that group wins. Remember, even though you may die, the things you said might provide valuable information to help your group win.

Battle
A Battle occurs between the Werewolves and the player they attack.  A player may only use a sign that is shown on their card.  The outcome is decided by one of the most basic 
systems known to man:
 Paper beats Rock
 Rock beats Scissors
 Scissors beat Paper   
 Evil wins all ties

Additional Roles
Dark Village includes several additional roles to enhance traditional gameplay. 
 Priest – Sees the role of a dead player each night
 Mason – Learns the identity of the other Mason 
 Dentist – Every third night chooses a player to silence for one day
 Star-Crossed Lover – Chooses a lover the first night.  If one dies, both die.
 Fortune Teller – learns one player’s role a night
 Hunter – Kills another player when he dies.
 Peeping Tom – May peek while the Evil players are “awake”
 Thief – Steals the role of one player the first night.  The player is dealt a new role.
 Lawyer – Chooses one person to save from lynching each night
 Sheriff – Vote to lynch counts twice
 Farmer – If the farmer dies, the Citizens have 3 days before they starve and die
 Scholar –  learns if an Evil is beside him
 Scientist – learns the number of  Evil alive
 Magician – Dies on second successful attack
 Village Idiot – May only speak gibberish
 Gravedigger – Buries a player alive during day
 Serial Killer – Kills at night on his own. Not part of either group.
 Psychiatrist – Suspects one player of being the Serial Killer each night. If correct, the Serial Killer is “cured” and becomes a normal Citizen.

Based on Mafia by Dimitry Davidoff and Werewolf by Andrew Plotkin

En Pointe Toujours !: Guadalcanal is a Tactical level World War 2 board-wargame of the En Pointe Toujours ! series.

En Pointe Toujours !: Guadalcanal includes:
- two A4 (GA, GB) maps - 29,7x21 cm
- two A2 (01, 02) overlays
- 108 die-cut counters
- One 24 pages rules and scenarios booklet

The game features WW2 Tactical Warfare in the Pacific, with five different scenarios:

     Tanambogo (3 scenarios campaign )
     Tasimboko
     Matanikau 


In each one, the first player is in charge of US soldiers, the other one plays the Japanese.

It is a stand-alone board-wargame - not an expansion.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Series: En Pointe Toujours! system (Vae Victis)

description:
In Die turbulente Zeitreise, the brilliant scientist Dr. Tempora has created a time machine, and the players are now going to go exploring the world's history.

In the first phase of the game, players are presented with board segments from different eras and they must determine in which order those segments should be organized to match history. In the second phase, players answer questions related to different eras of time, and in the third phase they must recall figures and items that they've experienced in the first two phases. Players score points each time they take a correct action, and whoever scores the most points wins.

It's all hands to the diving station as you make your way to Das versunkene Logik-Land. Watch out, though, as the residents of this underwater world will allow visitors to their mysterious city only if they manage to solve all of their tricky puzzles...

boardgame: Warhammer: Invasion – Battle for the Old World
description:
Game description from the publisher:

The Old World is a land of eternal war. As its rival races continue to combat each other, they must look for new tactics and new tricks or suffer defeat at the hands of enemies who have learned their standard tactics over years and years of battle. As we head deeper into the "Eternal War" cycle, Warhammer: Invasion - Battle for the Old World gives each of the races exactly what they need – new tricks and surprises – as it continues to reinforce the game's focus on a dynamic battlefield phase.

Battle of the Old World – which includes sixty new cards, three copies each of twenty different cards – introduces ten new units that each bring special abilities to force their enemies into difficult decisions and make combat more engaging. It also expands players' deck-building options with new Spells, Hexes, and Fortifications, as well as a new Greenskin legend certain to make an impact.
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)
Game: Warhammer Invasion – Living Card Game

description: In the atmospheric game world of Wettstreit im Hexenwald, players participate in the great contest of witches, collecting ingredients and brewing potions. Whoever can most skillfully use her goblins and collect the most ingredients will become champion apprentice. Be careful, though, as not all the witches are good...

boardgame: Tom & Tina: Das Tal der Tempel
description: In their second adventure game, Tom & Tina: Das Tal der Tempel, Tom and Tina head to Mexico to meet her uncle, who performs research on ancient temple pyramids. While traveling to the valley of the temples, they run across mounting evidence of a hidden Mayan treasure. Thus, the two of them want to reach Tina's uncle as quickly as possible so that they can begin the search for this treasure, but a few bad guys want to beat them to the find. An exciting race is now underway...

Each player has a set of chips numbered 2-9 which start in the circles bordering the edge of the grid.  Each grid square has a pair of numbers, each shown twice so as to be easily read by all players.  Played most like Chinese Checkers, the twist in Chekker Math is that a playing piece must be a factor of at least one number on each square on which it lands.  Thus, only a player's 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 8 chips may be played on the square showing "30" and "64".  The 7 and 9 chips may not be played on that square.

Children's quiz game. 300 questions on 60 cards. Play as long as you want and gain a card for each correct answer. Whoever has the most cards at the end, wins.

Also includes a book of facts that children might like to read.

Color Warz is a tactical game for 2 to 4 players. These paint brawls are good humoured contests rather than battles. The Khromaz, little creatures of different colors. Paint your opponents combatants to take control of them and gain the advantage! Choose your color, one of the five scenario to play and lead your Khromaz clan against your opponents. 
Your clan is composed of one Chief that can not change color, one paint Golem and between 2 and 5 combatants whose caste and availability depends the color you have chosen.
Place paint puddles on the board to protect your combatants and expand your network.

boardgame: Reise durch Ägypten (fan expansion for Ticket to Ride)
description:
Egyptian map for Ticket to Ride.

The map uses the original Ticket to Ride rules (i.e. no tunnels, ferries as introduced by Ticket to Ride Europe). I have not yet tinkered nor played this extension but it contains plenty of both long and short routes.
8 additional tickets (short-routes as in Mystery Train).

boardgame: Cuba: Region Occidental (fan expansion for Ticket to Ride)
description: The Cuban map for Ticket to Ride comes in a lengthier format, similar to that of the Italian map. It is 2 pages high and 4 pages wide. The board layout contains several long routes. Although several routes pass through water, the map works on the original Ticket to Ride rules.

boardgame: Wild Wild West (fan expansion for Ticket to Ride)
description:
Card expansion for Ticket to Ride

In addition to the original Ticket to Ride rules, the 30 Wild Wild West event cards are shuffled at the beginning of the game. Three cards are distributed to each player. Each player reviews his event cards and decides which ones to keep. A player must keep at least one card but may keep two or all three. 
Playing the event card will cost one action to play an event card.
There are variety of interesting events in this card deck like: "Double the value of any of your completed Destination tickets worth 6 points or less, at game's end" or "This card is revealed at game's end. If you are the first player with 2 or less trains score 10 additional points. Otherwise, lose 5 points. "

PNP files can be found in the files section of Ticket to Ride or
english version
french version
spanish version
cards back

This is a free Print and Play game designed for 2-6 players as either competitive or team play which plays in approximately 1hr.  It is a fantasy, tile placement game, where each player races to the center to get the treasure.

The placement of drawn tiles allows players to make a path for their heroes to get to the center tile where the treasure awaits or allows players to block their opponent's path.  On each turn players can play Event cards or draw and place monsters to also slow down their opponents or destroy them.  Loot cards can improve your chances to reach the center and win the game!  To gain Loot or event cards roll your attack dice to slay the monsters in your way!

Best of all, you can use Dungeon Crawler minis, or any fantasy miniatures you have on hand!

Available in November 2012, this version of the famous property game was developed from an original board designed by William Newman, son of Turing's mentor, Max, over sixty years ago. The spaces, Community Chest Cards, and Chance Cards tell the story of Alan Turing's life. Photos of the Turing family adorn the game board. Alan Turing's face is on the play money.

In Chicago, set in the United States during the age of Prohibition, the players are gang bosses who fight to control the city of Chicago. They try to earn money by controlling the areas of Chicago and building casinos and moonshine stills. Once Prohibition is lifted, the player with the most money wins – but before that happens, the player with the most money is arrested (like Al Capone) and half his funds claimed as punishment. Thus, you need to be second in order to win!

In a world filled with constantly fighting dragons, faeries, and other such creatures, smiles are rarely seen on the faces of everyday people – and this is where you, a circus owner, come in. You're determined to create the best of all circuses so that people may smile once again.

Cirque du Monstre is a deck-building game in which players use money to add monsters to their circus deck, then use the monsters to win fabled circus prizes. These prizes – effectively, the victory points – cannot be purchased directly, so you'll need to put on a good show in order to claim them.

Once the game ends, the player with the most prizes wins.

Even Steven is a light party game consisting of a card deck full of more or less complicated statements. Players around the table simultaneously choose "yes" (agree) or "no" (disagree) or "even". Once all players have revealed their choices, the ones who chose the same reply as the majority score one point – but if "yes" and "no" are tied, the ones who chose "even" each score two points.

Game loosely based on a game within a TV show. Players on the television show get to walk across the studio floor by identifying patterns. This game recreates that with player boards. Each player gets one and must place a chip in a circle that matches the pattern on the turn of a card. There are 40 cards so each game should be quite different. The aim is to form a path of chips from any colour to the opposite same colour. The first to do so, wins.

It can be played by individuals or as teams (if four are playing.

boardgame: The Candy Shop War Card Game
description:
The Candy Shop War Card Game is based on Brandon Mull’s pre-teen book series of the same name. It is a 2-player card and dice game that tasks players with collecting hidden Artifacts which are protected by nefarious Villains who will stop at nothing to keep you from what you seek. Luckily, you have been equipped with a deck of magical candy to fend them off and claim the treasure for your own.

On your turn, roll dice to create specific combos in order to activate your candies’ abilities and to accumulate Adventure tokens on a Location. The first player to get the necessary amount of Adventure tokens on a Location wins the Artifact hidden behind it and scores points. But beware the Villains that stand in your way! If there’s a Villain at your current Location, he can damage or incapacitate you. If your health falls to zero, you will lose points. If you manage to beat a Villain’s health to zero, you will receive Reward cards equal to its level. You only get to keep one Reward card, however, so choose wisely, whether it be extra points or potent power-ups. The first player to reach 15 points is declared the winner.

Legend - The 1000 Miglia Action Game is the second title of the Legend Series, covering the historical 1000 Miglia Race. This game is intended for all those gamers looking for a historically-accurate racing game,  but also characterized by simple rules and a streamlined mechanics. In few words an action game! The real key-point of this game is the scalable rules, suitable for small kids up to experienced gamers, especially with the special dice-pack add-on available on publisher's site.
You will have to face critical road conditions and winding roads, struggling to get the most out of your car, yet avoiding any unnecessary damage, to finish first in Brescia after 1000 miles of racing across Italy.
The most appreciated elements of its "big bro" Legend - The History of 1000 Miglia are featured also in The 1000 Miglia Action Game: 19 different car models, Car Performance Cards in the same colors of the real cars' cockpits, accurate stage layouts and the expected Made in Italy quality by WBS Games.

description:
Arboriculture and Formaggio were modules included in the original Kickstarter version of Tuscany that were eventually deemed “non-essential” for the compilation of Tuscany Essential. However, due to fan requests and curiosity, the publisher has made these modules (along with other modules) available for free in print-and-play format.

In addition, we granted a print-on-demand company, Print and Play Games, permission to make and sell a print-on-demand version of Arboriculture and Formaggio. (ships from the US).

---
The Viticulture: Arboriculture Expansion adds new "arbor" cards from which players can obtain olive oil, apples, and tomatoes. They are planted and harvested on the fields just like grapes, but the extra crops are processed on an extension to the vineyard mat. These additions add extra value to wine orders and can be used to improve worker morale. (The workers aren't happy that they have been given additional duties.)

This expansion essentially doubles the complexity of the game and adds about fifteen minutes to the overall playing time.

---
Trade for cows, make cheese, and add cheese to your wine orders for  in the Viticulture: Formaggio Expansion!

This expansion comes with 18 cows and six cheese cellar tokens. Cows can be bought or sold as an additional trade option at the “trade one for one” space on the Tuscany extended game board.

The mat has three new structures:


     Cheesemonger - may sell one cheese for 1VP on the “sell one wine token”
     Affinatore - allows you to choose one type of cheese and either not age it (so it doesn’t go rotten) or age it twice.
     Formager - once per year, you may discard one cheese token when you fill a wine order to gain VP’s up to the value of the cheese token. This can be up to 4 points, so as much as a wine order.

Game description from the publisher:

Cirplexed! is the quick-start game in which strategy comes full circle! Each tile features quarter circles of different colors. Each player creates her own game board by drawing and placing tiles, trying to create the most single-color circles. The game takes visual thinking and 360° of strategy to maximize your score.

Little Big Horn wasn't the only battle. Two other major battles (Rosebud Creek and Slim Buttes) and eight further battles were needed to suppress the Sioux and other tribal peoples who defended their homelands with both consummate skill and enduring courage.  A block styled war game that takes fog of war to another level through the use of command blocks and scouts on the board rather than full units as in most war games.

description:
This is an expansion to the fast paced card game Gibs.

New additions:

     4 new gib types with more advanced abilities
     more action cards to keep the deck balanced
     6 new white gem condition cards
     Cards to add a 5th player to the game


It's as simple as adding all the cards into the deck no messy choosing certain cards to remove.

You can buy Gibs: The Advanced Pack at this site https://www.thegamecrafter.com/games/gibs-the-advanced-pack

boardgame: Dust Tactics: KV-3 Heavy Walker – "Matrioshka / Babushka"
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Built up from the chassis of a captured Axis "Luther", reinforced, and equipped with the SSU's most effective armaments, the Dust Tactics: KV-3 Heavy Walker can be configured into either the Babushka, a lethal anti-infantry machine, or the Matrioshka, which provides SSU armored divisions with a solid antitank unit. Together, the Babushka and Matrioshka are capable of ripping apart troops and vehicles alike, making the KV-3 the proud heir to the long line of heavy tanks that have plagued Axis forces since the beginning of Operation "Barbarossa".

boardgame: Dust Tactics: Axis Armored Transport – "Prinzluther / Sturmprinz"
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Some troops are too valuable to be left exposed on a deadly battlefield. Enter the Dust Tactics: Axis Armored Transport, a valuable tactical unit in either of its two configurations. The Prinzluther is the game's first Command Vehicle. It can transport any unit with the Command Squad or NCO Command Squad skill, while its communications array allows such a squad to issue commands from within the transport's armored hull. Meanwhile, more heavily armed than the Prinzluther, the Sturmprinz can transport six soldiers on the inside with its Carry Capacity, and can still carry six more Tank Riders on the outside.

Paper Route is an analog re-creation of classic video game action. You will be scrambling to press buttons to complete combinations, only you will be using cards instead of a video game controller. Your goal is to deliver papers, avoid danger, and vandalize houses better, and faster than your opponent.

Paper Route is a turn-less "real-time" game. At the start of a round five houses are paired with five obstacles. Together, they form a sequence of buttons that players must play in order to score their points. Once both players agree, the round begins and the players flip cards, one at a time, from the top of their deck and play them on a house. The faster you can play, the better... but be careful! If you mess up, you lose a life and if you lose all your lives, your opponent wins!

Paper Route uses the Cardboard Arcade game system.  Cardboard Arcade consists of a deck of cards that represents a classic arcade controller.  The Controller Decks are generic and allow for the re-creation of many different arcade classics with a common component.

Paper Route and Cardboard Arcade have been released under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-Non-Commercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported license, http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/3.0/.  You are free to re-theme, modify, build-upon, and release your own games as long as you don't sell them without asking first. If you want to distribute your work in a commercial capacity, please don't hesitate to ask!

boardgame: 星杯传说: 跃动的幻之星尘
description: 《星杯传说——跃动的幻之星尘》 is a mini-expansion of Asteriated Grail. There are two brand new character (No.26 and No.29)and one corrected character (No.28) in the expansion.
publishers: 上海一逸文化传播有限公司

description:
Racing Dwarfs is a game of strategy and anticipation for 2-4 players

It’s widely known that the dwarfs who live in the mountains work hard extracting metals inside their dark chambers. But what most people ignore is their fondness of mine wagons races.

Today, some restless dwarfs have gathered for a new competition. In front of them, a little maze of spinning platforms traversed by rails awaits them to be crossed.

Will you be able to manage your team skilfully enough, by moving mine wagons and rotating platforms, to be the first to guide all your dwarfs beyond the opposite end of the maze?

GOAL

The players will try to lead their wagons beyond their opposite end of the board, by following the paths the platforms’ rails allow.

Lionheart vs. Saladin. As the Third Crusade drags on, the Crusaders can make no headway to reach the Levantine interior against Saladin’s highly mobile Arab army; but Saladin cannot generate enough power to break the Crusaders and recapture coastal cities. In the summer of 1191, Richard the Lionheart leads a Crusader army south along the coast, intending  eventually to turn east toward Jerusalem. Saladin, hoping to catch the Crusaders strung out on the march — as he had done four years earlier at Hattin — prepares to launch a strike near the ruined city of Jaffa. But, Richard, anticipating the attack, musters his army in a tight formation but continues to march nonetheless. On the morning of 7 September, Saladin strikes.

Arsuf is a contest of nimble Arab archers against stout Crusader men-at-arms, backed by the cream of European chivalry. Victory will go to the player who deploys to best utilize his strengths and take advantage of enemy weaknesses.

Arsuf uses the new Battles of the Medieval World system, a variation on the venerable Battles of the Ancient World system. The battle is dominated by masses of heavy cavalry and disciplined lines of heavily-armed and armored men-at-arms. Swirling around these forces are masses of foot- and horse-archers, whose purpose is to disrupt the heavy formations to make them easy prey for their counterparts. Using the classic Combat Results Table, the fighting is close and bloody until the morale of one side weakens. Once a line breaks, the victor must pursue ruthlessly to prevent a rally. Leaders enhance combat power and restore broken formations.

Game Contents:
•    17 x 22” map
•    100 die-cut counters
•    One Standard Rules booklet for this series
•    One Exclusive Rules booklet for this title

(source: Decision games website)
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Pea Ridge: St Louis, then Huzzah!
description:
A Union invasion of northern Arkansas ground to a halt in early 1862 due to logistical difficulties. The alarmed Confederates sent Earl Van Dorn to coordinate the fractious Southern forces to repel the Yankee invaders. Van Dorn, aggressive but careless, launched a quick offensive that nearly trapped Sam Curtis’ Union Army of the Southwest, until Curtis extricated his army and placed it in a well-entrenched position on the plateau of Pea Ridge. After their rapid march north, the Confederates were exhausted and out of supplies, but Van Dorn nevertheless set them on a grueling night march around Curtis’ position.

Pea Ridge uses the Musket & Saber system to accurately portray warfare during the American Civil War. All the primary facets of the muzzle-loading era are included. The combat system rewards intuitive play without the need for rifle-counting. There are substantial differences between infantry, artillery, and cavalry: Infantry, tough and resilient, bear the brunt of battle. Artillery can smash attacks or open holes in enemy lines, but is vulnerable and brittle. Cavalry is weak in stand-up combat, but crucial for pursuit and reconnaissance. All units are susceptible to rout when weakened, and the fortunes of war can intervene at any time, so players must maintain reserves. Leaders provide an important supplement to unit capabilities, and armies must protect their headquarters and logistical tail or risk deterioration of their mobility and combat power.

Winning the battle depends upon deployment, thoughtful maneuver to concentrate at the key points, the proper coordination of arms, careful use of leaders and special units, and an understanding of the strengths and weaknesses of each army.
family: Series: Musket & Saber Combat System (Decision Games)

description:
After capturing Vera Cruz and routing Santa Anna at Cerro Gordo in the spring, Winfield Scott’s US Army was poised to strike at Mexico City. Boldly cutting himself loose from his supply line—as Cortes had done three centuries earlier—Scott embarked upon a risky campaign: He had only slender political support; a single reverse—or an inordinately bloody victory—might have spelled the end of his campaign. Instead of an assault on Mexican fortifications east of the capital, Scott circled to the south where large lakes and a lava bed (the Pedregal) gave him numerous approach routes. The Mexicans quickly moved to block him, fortifying the most obvious routes, but they neglected to man the Pedregal itself, thinking it impassable to large units. The US forces, led by an excellent set of engineer officers, repeatedly found paths through the morass. But the US army was roughly half the size of the Mexican army and necessarily was divided. A resolute Mexican attack at the proper moment could have led to the destruction of Scott’s army.

Pedregal uses the Musket & Saber system to accurately portray warfare during the Mexican War. All the primary facets of the muzzle-loading era are included. The combat system rewards intuitive play without the need for rifle-counting. There are substantial differences between infantry, artillery, and cavalry: Infantry, tough and resilient, bear the brunt of battle. Artillery can smash attacks or open holes in enemy lines, but is vulnerable and brittle. Cavalry is weak in stand-up combat, but crucial for pursuit and the occasional charge against a broken foe. All units are susceptible to rout when weakened, and the fortunes of war can intervene at any time, so players must maintain reserves. Leader provide an important supplement to unit capabilities, and armies must protect their headquarters and logistical tail or risk deterioration of their mobility and combat power.

Winning a battle depends upon deployment, thoughtful maneuver to concentrate at the key points, the proper coordination of arms, careful use of leaders and special units, and an understanding of the strengths and weaknesses of each army.
family: Series: Musket & Saber Combat System (Decision Games)

description:
Your goal in Ninja Chess is the same as in regular Chess: Capture the opponent's king in order to win. The differences between the games include a smaller playing area, fewer pieces, and the inability to castle. The game has a historical Japanese theme, and players start with a Tonosama (King), an Okugata (Queen), a Samurai (Rook), a Ninja (Knight), and some Ashigaru (pawns), with the pieces being pretty detailed plastic pawns mounted on small bases.

Reimplements


    Chess

Simple Shogi contains two fast versions of Shogi, an abstract strategy game somewhat like Chess in that each player wants to capture the opponent's king, but all pieces can be promoted and captured pieces can be returned to the game board under the capturer's control. These versions are:


     Simple Shogi – On a 3x5 board, players start with only four pieces each, along with a gold and a silver general in hand. The game plays like Shogi except that pieces can be promoted only on the furthermost line.



     Rocket Shogi – On a 5x7 board, players start with eight pieces each instead of twenty. Otherwise, the game plays like normal Shogi.


Reimplements


    Shogi
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
In Jushimatsu ("Ten Sisters"), you are the owner of some cute finches. You and an opponent take turns placing one tile down at a time, and tiles depict 1-4 finches. You win when you line up more than ten finches of your own color in a vertical, horizontal or diagonal line.

The basic Jushimatsu set is for only two players, but you can combine a "finch and egg" set of another color to play with three or more players. ("Jushimatsu" is a kind of Japanese finch (Society finch or Bengalese finch), and the name means "ten sisters" because they are so friendly to each other like good sisters.

Jushimatsu has also been published under the name 10 Stars, with ceramic tiles instead of wooden ones and stars in place of birds.

Pincers is an abstract game for two players played on a 5x5 grid. Players each start with five crab pawns, one of which has a starfish printed on the bottom of it and another which has a pearl.

Players take turns moving one of crab at a time. If a crab is surrounded on both sides by the opponent's crabs, that crab is pinched and flipped over. If the flipped crab bears the pearl, the pinching player wins; if it has the starfish, the pinching player loses; and if the underside is blank, the crab is removed from the board and play continues.

Pincers includes a variant in which the pearl and starfish are visible.

Pumpkins have surprisingly small brain stems – non-existent even, you might say. As such they're not the brightest of creatures and they get in trouble easily. In Let's Pumpkin, you're trying to help this fragrant fruit find their way to safety, mostly so that you can plant them again later.

Starting with an array of face-down, vibrantly orange triangular tiles arranged in a circle, players take turns planting a pumpkin on an unrevealed tile – at which time the landscape beneath the tile is discovered, often to the pumpkin's detriment as each terrain tile hides a trap. The pumpkins can catch fire, freeze, be sucked up by tornado, and so on. To counter the devastating effects of these traps and save the pumpkins, a player can use tokens acquired from burning or frozen terrain; alternatively, a player can use these tokens against opponents, really grinding their gourds by strengthen traps that affect them.

Whoever manages to plant the last pumpkin, whether owned or already on the game map, wins!

Taken from the designer's game description...

This game arises out of many long nights of playing other games that took way too long and started way to many fights. Playing a board game involves multiple players, each looking to win. The resulting competition can devolve into incredible displays of lunacy. Nit-picking over the rules, collusion against specific players because of grudges carried over from other games, and even fights over bending or missing pieces.

This game harnesses all of this angst into a game in and of itself!

In this game, you move on a board that's only 10-spaces long, trying to be the first person to get to the end. You do this by playing specific cards on your turn, each representing things that might happen during a typical board game. Watch out though, because your opponents will be trying to get there first as well. Cards like "Analysis Paralysis", "Bathroom Break", "Blame-Shifter", and good old-fashioned "Cheating" will no-doubt bring back memories of past board-game fights. Who knows, maybe you'll all get along next time!

boardgame: Military Modelling Guide to Solo Wargaming
description:
The book contains a variety of information and ideas for solo wargaming from small scale skirmishes to large campaigns.  Each scenario contains basic rules so that they are playable straight away without reference to another ruleset.  However, the book also provides ideas that can be applied to whichever ruleset the reader prefers.

From the back cover:

"Solo wargaming is growing rapidly in popularity.  In this book, Stuart Asquith, editor of Practical Wargamer magazine sets out the reasons for solo play and its attractions and advantages.

The book covers all manners of actions from small-scale skirmishes through to campaigns, and the historical spread of the contents is extensive.  Other aspects of solo wargaming discussed include chance cards, terrain, the unpredictable enemy, maps and map making, supplies, the weather and postal play.

Illustrated with photographs and line drawings, Military Modelling Guide to Solo Wargaming is the definitive book on the subject."

boardgame: The Game of Alice in Wonderland
description:
This is the first game ever published based on the book Alice's Adventures in Wonderland.

From the Rules Card:

The Game of Alice in Wonderland

This game contains 52 cards divided into three sets.

SET. I. 16 Picture Cards numbered from 1 to 16 with a star in the corner.

SET. II. 16 Picture Cards without star,  numbered 1 to 16.

SET. III. 20 number cards numbered from 1 to 20.

The two sets of picture cards form pairs by matching their numbers.

Each set takes by its number. The higher cards taking the lower ones. Star cards take each other according to their numbers, and both the other sets, whether their numbers are high or low. Plain Picture Cards take each other according to their numbers, and all number cards. Number cards take each other according to their numbers.

Elaborately illustrated boards table-game featuring the voyage from Amsterdam Central Station via Paris and Genua to the Dutch East Indies. Illustrations featuring trains, Eiffeltower, passengerships and all Islands of the Indonesian Archipelago. Gamers have to pass 68 numerical stations before arriving the final destination. Boardgame with rather complicated rules and touristinfo. about the Dutch East Indies. Drawn and designed by Kees Mandos, dutch painter, draughtsman and graphic artist, copy signed and dated in the stone at tail margin. Very fine copy of this tablegame published under nazi oppression of Holland 1940-1945.

boardgame: Mage Knight Board Game: The Lost Legion Expansion
description:
Stories about General Volkare and his Lost Legion are whispered amongst the Atlanteans – some in hope, all with at least a sliver of fear. Now with word of what appears to be Volkare and his Lost Legion reappearing and yet another new Mage Knight to contend with, the scattered forces across the land are deciding which side is likely to be victorious. Are the Mage Knights the lesser of evils, or is Volkare the true savior they have been waiting for?

Continuing the saga of the worldwide acclaimed hit Mage Knight Board Game, designer Vlaada Chvatil and WizKids present Mage Knight Board Game: The Lost Legion, an expansion that offers players a new Mage Knight — Wolfhawk — and also adds a new foe for the Mage Knights: the mysterious and powerful General Volkare! Included in the expansion set are new location tiles, enemy/ruin tokens, Unit cards, Advanced Action cards, Artifact cards, Spell cards and a rulebook expansion that features new game scenarios and rules to support play for five players.

Whether you and your fellow Mage Knights decide to work together or at cross purposes this expansion supports new play styles and tactics and a new foe that can challenge even the most veteran players.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Kenakalan is a unique set collection family game.  It features themed tour cards showing photographs of Bali, wild cards and wise and wicked monkey cards.  Kenakalan means 'mischief' in Indonesian.  There are 98 cards total.  The number of cards in play is adjusted to suit the number of players.  Play time is between 15 and 30 minutes.

Each player has a different-colored shop front on which to collect finished sets (tours).  The tours must be different in number and theme.   The first player to complete all of their sets is the winner.

Players can take one action during their turn.  At the beginning of the game, their choices are rearranging or taking cards from a central display of five cards or taking the top card off the deck.  Finished sets can be laid down at the end of a player’s turn.  As the game progresses, players can use their action to pick up a completed tour.  On a subsequent turn they can lay it back down with additional cards to increase the size of that tour.

Wise monkeys, drawn from the deck, allow players to take two different actions in their turn, such as rearranging and then taking from the display.  Wise monkeys can be used immediately or kept for a more convenient time.  Wicked monkeys can also appear from the deck but cause immediate havoc, for example by stealing a wild card from a player’s shop front.  Holding fewer cards protects players from certain wicked monkey effects, in which case the monkey is passed to the first eligible player. However, to win you often have to take the risk of holding a large hand.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

boardgame: The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game – The Hobbit: On the Doorstep
description:
Game description from the publisher:

The Hobbit: On the Doorstep is the second Saga Expansion for The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game, continuing the story begun in The Hobbit: Over Hill and Under Hill. Players directly relive the exciting events of the beloved classic, The Hobbit. Encounter giant spiders, burgle treasures from the mighty Smaug, and fight the Battle of Five Armies. The Hobbit: On the Doorstep – which includes 165 new cards, including three copies of each player card – introduces three new scenarios, five new heroes, and player cards all pulled from the pages of the second half of The Hobbit!
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)
Game: The Lord of the Rings – The Card Game (LCG)

description:
A double-nine set of dominoes is used to build a board of 13x8 squares. (There's also a variant for double-six.) Initially, only the outer tiles are placed, and 13 pawns are lined up on the farthest rank. The goal is to get all pawns into the nearest 2 ranks by placing tiles that permit pawns to cross the board.

Pawns are moved orthogonally through a path of pips whose sum is exactly 9. Pawns cannot enter nor pass squares occupied by other pawns.

Young children's game related to both Snap and Memory but with differences.

The game has 72 tiles. 36 of them are all the same shape and feature various images. The other 36 tiles match the object on the same shape tiles but these tiles are not rectangular, they are loosely the shape of the object in question.

Place all the same shape tiles into the cardboard container. Lay out all the other tiles face-up around the table. At the word go, players hunt through the container looking for a tile that matches (the image) any exposed tile on the table. First to get three matches wins the round and cries "Halt!".

The same-shaped tiles are perfect for also playing a straight-forward Memory game or a game of Snap.

Children's game to help them learn to tell the time. The main game is a race to complete the jigsaw that makes up the board. Depending on age, allow a player 1 or 2 minutes to finish the jigsaw. The fastest wins.

The jigsaw features a clock in the centre with moveable hands, and around the clock are activities associated with the time of day. In the second game, a parent can ask such things as "When do you leave for school?" and if a player gets it right, then a point is scored. At the game end, the player with the most points wins.

In Karolinchen – the third game in Zoch's "worm family" after Da ist der Wurm drin and Streifen Toni – the player worms must crawl and eat through the orchard, eating different fruits to grow faster than their friends.

In the game, each player starts with the head of a worm (with a pawn on it) and a different color of fruit, plus a set of cards with a certain number of fruits on one side and a section of the growing worm on the other side. In addition to this, the game includes six dice that show the colors of six different fruits.

At the start of a round, all six dice are rolled in the middle of the table. On a turn, a player picks dice matching his current color and places them onto the fruits of his card. Non-matching dice are available for the next player(s). If on a player's turn no matching dice are available, he may roll these dice again and (perhaps) pick them up. When all the dice have been claimed by the players, their worm pawn "eats", moving forward to the fruit on which the last die was placed, then all the dice are rolled again to start a new round.

When a player places a die onto the last fruit of his current card, he adds this card to his worm, then chooses one of his remaining cards and places it in front of his worm. The player whose worm has eaten its way through all colors first wins the game.

The game of guessing the answers to funny job descriptions.

The game has 198 riddles, each one relating to a certain profession.

The person to the left of the player whose turn it is, slides from under the shuffled deck of cards (so that no-one can read it as they would have done should he draw it from the top of the deck. Each card has 6 riddles. The reader of the card rolls a die and must read out the job against the number rolled. The guesser tries to answer correctly. If he does, his piece is moved up the board. If wrong, the card is placed on the top of the stack and it is now the turn of the next player, and so on.

The first player to get to the finish line (Ziel) on the board, wins.

The object is to be the first reaching target zone on the little game board by answering most questions correctly.
There are 2 question and 3 variants for each question on one side of the card. Currect player draws a card and hide the answers. Other players show answers simultaneously;
Players who answer correctly scores 1 point and move their pawns one point further on little game board. 
If leading player moves upon a 'specail' space on the board: Last and second last Player run a 'Rock,Paper,Scissors' competition to make a step ahead.
Who finishes first on the game board is the winner.

Topic: Cars

The object is to be the first reaching target zone on the little game board by answering most questions correctly.
There are 2 question and 3 variants for each question on one side of the card. Currect player draws a card and hide the answers. Other players show answers simultaneously;
Players who answer correctly scores 1 point and move their pawns one point further on little game board. 
If leading player moves upon a 'specail' space on the board: Last and second last Player run a 'Rock,Paper,Scissors' competition to make a step ahead.
Who finishes first on the game board is the winner.

Bionic is a paragraph driven solo adventure game set in an alternate future where those with the resources have become half-machine. Super corporations rule the world from elaborate orbital stations while gangs, governments and citizens struggle to survive on the ever worsening surface of Earth.

In this game you create an Android who is employed by the largest augmentation corporation in the universe - Cyber Tech Revolutions Ltd. Each game you select a Contract from the Data Archives to complete, which will have you exploring the city of Kinacha (divided into Upper Kinacha and Lower Kinacha) completing the tasks set forth by your employer.

The game utilizes a roll and keep mechanic. For any given task there will be a difficulty number and based on your skills and attributes you will roll a certain number of 10-sided dice and then may select to keep a certain number. If your final result is above the TN you act recklessly, below the TN is cautious and matching the TN is the ideal outcome.

The game has been divided into 5 Chapters for ease of printing and playing. Each Chapter will slowly add more mechanics to the game building on the core of Chapter 1 and the Contracts in each successive Chapter build on the story of those occurring before it:

Chapter I is an introduction to the Bionic world and in particular the city of Kinacha.

Chapter II introduces a more fluid Encounter deck (with more cards that can be permanently removed as well as cards that only get added when you achieve certain goals), a new game board (Lower Kinacha) and allow access to the Underground via the Underground Access Status card. Chapter II focuses on the gang war in Lower Kinacha.

Chapter III takes place in the Underground proper and introduces a secondary Encounter Deck and a secondary Item Deck for this purpose.

Chapter IV expands upon what is already in place, and mostly adds a large number of new contracts to move the story towards the conclusion. This story arc features many decision points in the Contracts that will affect future Contracts.

Chapter V is the conclusion, and knowing what the additional components are would be a spoiler!

Publisehr Blurb:

Lynn Steiner is the second daughter of the Steiner house, one of the two main Black Sun families. Raised in privilege to be a leader, pride prevents her from leaving her business to agents and prefers to act directly accompanied by her servants. She is in almost superhuman physical condition, and has access to the largest collection of supernatural artifacts in existence on Gaia.

Artifacts: Lynn can take up to two Advantage cards, whose combined cost is 25 levels or less, without having to pay for these levels.

Natural Born Leader: During the Recovery Phase, you may remove an Action Point from a friendly unit in Lynn's Control Zone and give one Action Point to another friendly unit with whom Lynn has Line of Sight. This ability cannot be used on Undead units.
 House Steiner: Whenever Lynn receives damage, all friendly units in her Control Zone gain Haste Lv. 1. Limit: Once per turn. This ability cannot be used on Undead units.
 X-Calibur (Ki, Attack) - +4 Attack
 Arrow of Destiny (Ki, Charge) - +2 Attack. This Charge allows Lynn to move up to 16 inches, and ignores the target unit's armor.
 Mirror Image (Ki, Effect) Lynn uses her second Defence value. Whenever an attack targeting Lynn misses, she can reactively perform an Attack or Charge with no cost against the attacker, even if she had already moved this turn. While this ability is active, Lynn cannot Dodge.
 Exaltation (Magic, Effect) Remove a negative status marker from a friendly unit in Lynn's Control Zone to put a positive status marker of your choice on Lynn. Limit: Once per turn.

Balancing game. To set up for play, you must assemble a palm tree and place the monkey on the point of the top of the tree. He will balance there very precariously.

His limbs are so arranged that you can slide one of your plastic bananas on to a couple of limbs. Every success scores you a point. If succesful, you get to balance another and so on until you make him fall or you drop a banana or you get all 16 bananas on which would score you bonus points.

You play three rounds and the one who has accumaleted the most points, wins.

A card game lonked to characters in a children's TV show in Germany. The most famous character in it, Bernd the Bread, is illustrated on the box.

The six chilipower tiles are shuffled and placed face down. Players are dealt some cards that will have a number on each one, from -2 to +4. A start card is revealed and in your turn you play a card and add it to the start card, announcing the new total. Now next player adds a card and reveals the new total (that is, Start card + previous player's card + his new card). And so the game progresses. If any player takes the total to 13 or more, that player takes one of the 6 Chilipower tiles. The number of chillies on it is bad news because the winner will be the one with the fewest chillies on his chilipower tiles (they range from 1 to 6).

After taking his chilipower tile, the previous round's cards are collected up and put on the bottom of the stack and a new start card is revealed and you play as before. When the last chilipower tile is taken, the game ends and the winner is the one with the fewest chillies.

A game about colors for small children. The Yeti wants to color up his white surroundings - fortunately, he discovers a color lab. 
The game consists of tiles which have two colors on one side and the color you get when you mix the two on the other. These tiles are laid out in a circle (2 color side up) and the Yeti is placed onto one of them. The players then take turns rolling a die. On a result of one, two or three the Yeti moves forward the respective number of tiles. Then the player has to say which color is on the other side of the tile. If wrong, nothing happens. If correct, the player takes a token of the color s/he named. If the result is a star, the player can place the Yeti onto any tile. The first player who gets three tokens of the same color or four all in all is the winner. 
Advanced rules suggest to flip the tiles around and let the kids name the two colors necessary to mix the color they see.

Game description from the publisher:

The wicked witch Esmelda has cast her magic stones again! In Spooky Spells players think up a hilarious spell for one unlucky player to perform – Buy dinner for everyone? Sing an impromptu Russian ballad? – then start playing cards to keep the magic stones faaar away!

The rules are simple and fit for all ages. Each turn, a player with magic stones gets to play a spell card, which will send her stones all over the board. A variety of fun and mini-game spell cards keep the actions new and exciting – but once you run out of spells, the stones might come pouring in. Get stuck with the most at the end, and you'll be the one performing the spooky spell!

boardgame: Agricola: All Creatures Big and Small – More Buildings Big and Small
description: Agricola: All Creatures Big and Small - More Buildings Big and Small, an expansion for the standalone two-player game Agricola: All Creatures Big and Small, adds 27 new special buildings to the base game as well as a fifth Stall tile and a farm expansion. These tiles offer players new ways to keep and breed animals, and allow them to expand their farms in interesting and innovative ways.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Snozzle is a 4-player card game that utilizes a Pinochle deck, bidding, trump suits, taking tricks, and teams (two people per team, the person across the table from you is your teammate). The goal of the game is to be the team that reaches 52 points. In the even that both teams reach 52 tricks during the same hand, the team that won the bid during the final hand is the winner.

Using a Pinochle deck (with no Jokers), the dealer deals 3 cards face down to each person, clockwise starting with the player to the left, until all cards have been dealt. Each player then picks up their hand and sorts the cards by suit and value. Jacks in the Trump Suit (known as "Right" Jacks) are the highest value, Jacks in the same color but different suit as Trump (known as "Left" Jacks, or "Bowers") are second highest value cards, and opposite colored Jacks (no special name) are valued as normal. In all other suits, the Ace is the highest value with 9 being the lowest.

Each person bids only once, starting with the player to the left of the dealer, as an estimate of how many tricks they think they can take as a team (do not declare a suit, only a number). If the person bidding is the first person of the team, they may bid a "signal bid" to help their teammate bid higher (see below for signal bids). You may only bid if you bid higher than the person who bid before you. Otherwise you must say "pass." After all 4 players have bid or passed, the highest bidder wins the power to pick the suit of trump (which affects the value of Jacks and of the trump suit). The bidder then starts by playing a card. If "no trump" is chosen, aces are the highest value of card, but the person to the right of the winning bidder starts. Unlike other games, such as Bridge, all suits are considered equal, except for that of trump, and the "left" Jacks.

As the first card is played, each player must follow suit if able (note: "Left" Jacks become the suit of trump and no longer qualify as being of their original suit. These Jacks are called "Bowers"). After each player has played a card, the highest card in the suit wins the trick. If you are unable to follow suit, you may play trump, which automatically becomes the winning suit. A higher trump card can win, but all players must follow the suit that was lead.

After the trick is taken, the player who took the trick may lead the next trick with the card of his/her choice. If you win the number of tricks you bid (or more), you team is awarded a point for each trick taken. The team that did not win the bid also receives a point for each trick taken. If the team that won the bid does not take enough tricks equal to or greater than the number bid, they are "set." They receive a negative score equal to their bid. The other team is scored as normal.

Signal Bids:
1-4 (I have X number of aces in my hand. If you have more than 4 aces, you should either bid 4 to signal aces or bid 6+ to win the bid and possibly choose "no trump" as the suit of power.)
5 (I have at least 1 black and at least 1 red jack in my hand. This shows trump power/support to your partner if they are to win the bid and choose the suit of trump.)
6-12 (I can take X number of tricks, but can only do so in my suit of choice. This is a bid that attempts to win the bid rather than signal your teammate. However, a bid of 6 shows your partner that you may have some Jacks, but they are of one color and you don't have much supporting power)

A winning bid is usually a 7 or 8 or an occasional 9 or 10 bid. 6 bids rarely win, and 11/12 bid hands are difficult to come by, though entirely possible.

Historical note: I have never found the name or rules for this game to be published in any book or website, and I have only ever found players of this game in the state of Minnesota, usually by elderly people in rural areas. It may be of Scandinavian or German heritage.

Are you ready for an exciting electoral campaign? In Si la tendance se maintient simulates, in 2-5 hours, a full electoral campaign, day by day. Players represent political party leaders in a generic Western parliamentary democracy using pure proportional representation.

During the course of the game, players will manage a hand of cards that they, over 28 campaign days, use to attack each other, as they try to increase their voting intentions at the expense of other players. Taking into account their voting intentions and the strength of their partisans, players elect a certain number of MPs. A majority player will automatically win, but a minority situation will allow players to form a coalition.

Designed by a well-known member and executive of the New Democratic Party of Canada, Si la tendance se maintient, a French-only game, includes several appendix aiming at generating a discussion about the nature of politics, and of its institutions.

An educational game about works councils in the Netherlands.

Players are candidates for a works council. On their turn, players will roll the die to move forward on a track of experiences in a works council, and encounter various events. The first player to reach the end and get elected wins the game.

A family game where players travel around Europe and export their products to each country.

Players roll the die to move around the game board. They first have to capture the European flag from the middle of the board, or from a fellow player. Once one has the flag, one can proceed to open up the borders of each European country in order. When you open up a country, an export document is revealed, and the opening player can score points by handing in the goods pictured.

An angling game, where players try to catch fish with magnetic fishing rods. On each turn, players are allowed to catch one fish only - if multiple fish "bite" on your rod, they have to be thrown back. The player who caught the most valuable fish wins the game.

Similar to Gone Fishin' (perhaps an earlier version?), but without the junk you can fish up and this has a much shallower pond to fish from.

A simple children's game, where the players try to be the first to get all 7 dwarves to their table. On their turn, players spin a spinner which tells them to either place a new dwarf, steal a dwarf from others or discard one of their dwarves.
family: Folk Tales & Fairy Tales: Snow White

description:
Players explore the best ways to process dredging spoils. The game was created for the dutch ministry of water management by the dutch organization for applied research TNO.

On their turn, players place a tile on the board in Carcassonne fashion. However, to put a meeple on the tile, one has to answer a question about the dredging process. The player who scored the most points, and knows most about dredging, wins the game.

Players collect letters in a roll-and-move fashion. The first player to complete the word "Verkade" wins the game.

The letters are hidden face-down on the game board, and players roll the dice to move and collect them.

A combination of dominoes, memory and a quiz game. The game consists of square cards, each showing two halves of different pictures.

On their turn, players turn over two cards. If they match and form a complete picture, the player has to choose and answer one of the four quiz questions. If he knows the correct answer, he can either keep the two cards, or try and open up a third card which then has to match to one of the ends of his chain. The player who collected the most cards at the end wins the game.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
This game consists of a deck of cards, with each card describing a small mini-game that can be played with next to no pieces. These games range from deduction, bluffing to just plain silliness.

On their turn, players turn over a card and then play the short game described there. First player to win three of these mini-games wins.

The goal of the game is to throw those combinations with five dice that match the ones on the common cards in the middle of the table.  Each turn, the dice are passed to the next player, wgo can choose which Dice to reroll. Whomever collects most points wins the game.
A Dutch Memory variant, in which players try to collect matching DIY items. However, one should not match the same illustrations, but rather tools that go together.

Tactical level wargame based on the anime series Mobile Suit Z Gundam. The game focuses on squad tactics of mobile suits in combat, rather than one-on-one battles. This game is an updated version of Gundam 0080 Cyclops Attack, with updated rules and units from UC 0087 (the Gryps War period).

Players control eighteen-meter armored humanoid-shaped robots called mobile suits.
Each counter represents an individual mobile suit. Data cards explain different units and their corresponding abilities.

Each turn, players secretly select the tactics they will employ per platoon, and then units alternately activate by platoon. Most tactics deal with movement or combat. The winner of the battle is determined by destruction of opposing mobile suits.

The game include some scenarios from Zeta Gundam, but the main draw is a campaign game, with the players having the ability to build and customize units sent into combat. In the campaign game, players also manage their pilots in addition to the mobile suits. The pilots’ abilities can grow through combat experience. Points scored in battle allow for replacement of older and weaker mobile suits by newer models.

In Do You Know Your Neighbors?, between 5 and 12 people  play the role of a Kind, Wicked or Fickle neighbor.

The object of the game is to resolve enough Situation Cards so that your team's score pile has a total of 15 points.  The catch, however, is that you do not know who is part of your team as all identities are hidden. Each resident has a vested interest in the town resolving situations their way. There is strength in numbers, so players must also attempt to discover who among them is working for or against their efforts.

During each round of play, players may question another player with Clue Cards, giving them insight into that neighbor’s identity. Then, splitting into 2 task forces, players encounter Situation Cards with various point values. Kind or Wicked Deed Action Cards are played, like a vote, to resolve a given situation.  How everyone responds to these situations is up to them—and it's done in secret!

With enough careful sleuthing, you can figure out who are friends and who are foes.   Identifying a player's identity from their Neighbor Card prevents them from identifying others and gives you a bonus. So identify them before they identify you!
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Game description from the publisher:

In Der Kleine Prinz: Planetenwanderer, the serpent and its evil helpers – the darklings – are darkening one planet after another, bring the residents misfortune. The players must help the little prince find the darkened planets in space and get rid of the evil power of the snake. Whoever has a good memory will find the planet with the help of the gyroscope has a good chance of breaking the power of the serpent and winning the game. Hurry, though, as the snake is already gathering its forces...

Publisher Blurb:

Sarah is the heiress of the once prestigious house Reindhold. Her supernatural abilities manifested at a young age, causing her uncle Alfred to use all of his influence and power to get her taken in by the church as a Saint. Since then she has worked hard to master her innate skills and use them to support the Nathaniel cause. She hopes this way she can help as many people as possible.

Summoner Level: 1 Affinity: Light.  Mist of the Hallowed: Any ranged attacks against a friendly unit within Sarah's Control Zone suffer -1 Attack.
 Nathaniel's Incense (Magic, Effect) - Units within Sarah's Control Zone recover 2 LP each during the Recovery Phase, and cannot be Stunned due to a Crash. Upkeep: o
 Stigmata (Magic, Effect, Reactive) - When a friendly Unit within Sarah's Control Zone would lose LP due to their own ability or rules, Sarah loses the LP instead.
 Nathaniel's Hand (Magic, Effect) - Magic and Ki abilities that are being Upkept by units within Sarah's Control Zone are cancelled. Effect 16. Limit: Once per turn.

In Confusing Sun, players compete to spot particular combinations of flames and UFOs near the sun before anyone else can.

The basic side of the game board has spaces numbered 1-10, and at the start of the game you place one card next to each space; cards show 1-3 flames, 1-3 UFOs, and some number of planets (including zero). On a turn, the active player  rolls the black die and red die, then places the flame/UFO indicator card so that red is associated with flames and black with UFOs (or vice versa). This indicator card shows how many flames and how many UFOs players need to spot – for example, 1 flame and 3 UFOs.

For all cards that show precisely this combination of icons, the player sums the numbers (1-10) next to these cards and yells out a total. The first player to do so claims these cards, which are worth one point each if they don't have planets. (If a card has planets, it's worth that many points.) One side on each die shows a star; when a star is next to an icon, that icon is ignored. If stars are next to both icons, you total the numbers next to cards that bear planets.

After replacing any cards claimed, the next player rolls the dice. The game continues until someone has twenty points; this player wins! For a more challenging game, use the reverse side of the game board, which has spaces numbered 11-20.

The game consists of cards showing different animals doing actions (turning around, jumping on one leg, clap hands etc.). Players take turns flipping over two cards from the draw pile and placing them in a row. As soon as a player spots that there are three of the same animal in the row, she has to make the respective animal sound. If three of the same action can be seen, the player must perform the action. The first player to do that wins the three cards (or shares them with others who were as fast). When all cards are used up, whoever won most cards is the winner.

In World in Four, players try to assemble sets of nation cards by grabbing them from both the table and opponents. Each player starts with twelve cards in hand (or ten, depending on the number of players) and fourteen cards are arranged in a grid on the table.

On a turn, the active player reveals one card from the table to all players; if she thinks she can make a set of four cards (including the revealed one), she attempts to do so, claiming them as a set if she does so and adding one card from her hand to the face-down array if she fails. (She also turns the revealed card face down again.) The player then identifies an opponent and names a country or number. If that opponent has such a card, he must hand it over to her and she takes another turn; otherwise her turn ends and the player she attempted to steal from takes the next turn.

Once all cards have been played as sets, the game ends. Sets numbered 6-13 are worth that many points, the South Korea set is worth 10 points, and all other sets are worth 5 points each. The player with the highest score wins.

A-Mart – subtitled Arithmetic Mart – has you shopping for food in the supermarket, but your goal is not to grab all the food that you can, but rather to remember how much food you actually bought. Oh, and you're a porcupine.

All players start at the entrance of the supermarket. On a turn, you roll the die, move that many spaces on the track, then take 1-2 food items from the shelves next to you on the movement track. Supermarket employees help you take more goods (so helpful!), while porcupine bullies force you to toss an item previously placed in your cart. Land on another player and you take something from them, potentially messing up both your counts. Some food cards – being coupons, I suppose – subtract from your total; others double the total.

Whoever reaches the end of the track first collects five bonus points, and the game ends. Players then announce the total amount of goods they've collected. If correct, they add 20 points to their score; if they're off, they lose 10-50 points depending on how far they're off the mark. Whoever then has the highest score wins.

The Mindflex platform (moving a ball by increasing an air stream which varies based upon your concentration measured via a headset) is enhanced to allow two-players simultaneously. This version allows two players to play simultaneously either in a versus or co-operative mode.

Users can play a variety of games in solo, competitive or co-operative mode.  Game Menu:
Freestyle (solo)
Power Push (competitive 2-player)
Mental Marathon 2.0 (solo/competitive/co-op)
Race the Lights (solo/competitive)
Cortex Collision (solo/competitive)
Basketblaster (solo/competitive)

From back of Box: For the first time, test your mental power directly against another player! Face off against another player in a mental shoving match! Use the power of your thoughts to force the ball into your opponent's end zone to win!  Show off your mental gymnastics as you race through a customizable obstacle course - whether playing solo or co-op mode!

The object of the game is to capture 4 different Squinkies cards.  Cards can be captured by landing on Squinkies spaces, or by winning Dice Battles against other players (when both land on a Dice Battle space), or by winning Squinkies Battle! spaces (by flicking your piece and moving another player's piece off the board).  There are 6 different groups of Squinkies: Ninjas, Aliens, Warriors, Sports, Monsters, Skaters.  They are on different color backgrounds.  Once you have cpatured 4 different Squinkies cards, return to the Skull Cavern Dispenser in the center.

boardgame: Proxy Wars: Oath Bound Expansion Set
description:
Proxy Wars is an expandable two-player game in a contemporary but slightly fictional setting. Players represent a battle-ready faction and use their agents to fight for control of a customizable board, which is set up individually each game. To win, agents must occupy valuable tiles worth victory points (VPs) or wipe out enemy agents or do both.

Proxy Wars is a full-fledged strategy card-based board game, and the game mechanisms include tile-placing (the cards form the board) and deck-building elements. You are to decide how to best deploy your faction's power and agents to outwit your opponent with ingenious cutting, positioning and attacking strategies.

Proxy Wars: Oath Bound Expansion Set contains six GreenEngage and five MegaCorp cards as well as scenario cards and a scenario booklet.

Game description from the publisher:

In Android: Netrunner - What Lies Ahead, the first Data Pack in the "Genesis Cycle", its sixty cards (three copies each of twenty different cards) expand the base game, adding muscle and theme to each of the Core Set's seven different factions. The Corps each receive a new agenda and flavorful piece of ice while the Runners launch into the ongoing cyberstruggles with new icebreakers and support cards. Additionally, the Anarch and Haas-Bioroid factions each pursue new interests with a new identity card, and the new Anarch identity – Whizzard – provides Anarch players a whole depth of new strategies based around trashing the cards they access.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)

description:
Fairytale Pyramideto is the second expansion of Pyramideto game.

Fairytale Pyramideto includes 12 cards of a dozen of czech fairytale characters - like the Little Mole, fairy Amalka, Ferda the Ant or Rumcajs the Robber.

Cards have a typical six-color frame, name, pyramid form and the sign - fairytale crown.

Children/players are invated to add their own pictures on the card. (This is not necessary for the game.)

boardgame: Brunhilde: Legend of the Galactic Heroes
description:
Tactical level wargame based on Japanese space opera novel and animation, Legend of the Galactic Heroes. This game covering prelude battles of the Amlitzer campaign.

Each players control one star fleet belong either Galactic Empire or Free Planets Alliance.
Each unit represent group of one type of space warship. The units organize to certain divisions, designation by scenario. On the game, players manage movement and combat per each division as same as the unit of the standard wargame, and divisions can not divide or regroup in the play.

Winner determined by eliminate of opponent units.
The game include nine scenarios.
