Setting: Legend of the Galactic Heroes

description:
Airlines Europe: The Presidents, a promotional item that debuted at Spiel 2012, lets players bluff a bit more in Airlines Europe as to which airlines they most want to back with their stock.

At the start of the game, each player is dealt two random president tokens from the ten available, with each token matching one of the ten airlines in the game. The players look at these tokens in secret and keep them hidden until they choose to reveal them.

During a payout round, when players score points based on the stock they hold in a particular airline, if a player is tied with one or more opponents in a stock for which he holds the president token, that player can choose to reveal the token, breaking the tie in his favor. Any opponent(s) tied with this player are bumped down to the next payout level for this airline. The token remains face-up until the end of the game and comes into play in each subsequent payout, if needed.

An unrevealed president token is worth one point at the end of the game.

Monuments: Ewiger Ruhm ("Eternal Glory"), a promotional item given away at Spiel 2012, consists of a short scoretrack counting from 1-14, and four tokens in each color, with a set of tokens showing a helment, a ship, a scroll, and a monument. Whenever a player builds or expands a monument, he uses these tokens to track the highest levels of his holdings in these four categories. If, for example, his first monument is of five cards with three ships and two helments, he'd place the monument token on five, the ship token on three, and the helmet token on two. The scoretrack always reflects the highest value you have reached ever in a game, so your token does not go down when your monuments degenerate.

In each category the player with the highest token gets additional victory points at the end of the game. In case of a tie the one is better, who has reached the highest level first. The runner-up gets a smaller reward.

Knights of Ten: Battle Ready includes four copies of three new cards that can be added to your Knights of Ten deck. These cards are:


     Sorceress: Takes horde to zero.
     Take the Donkey: Take the donkey.
     Dragon Slayer: The dragon cannot be played this hand.

Knights of Ten: Strategic includes four copies of three new cards that can be added to your Knights of Ten deck. These cards are:


     Negate: Negate the last card played.
     Draw Two: Draw two cards.
     Mirror: Copy the last card played.

Zooloretto: Goodie-Box consists of five mini-expansions for Zooloretto or Aquaretto, with four of the expansions appearing previously in Zooloretto/Aquaretto expansion packs published by Rio Grande Games, but not published in Germany. The expansions are:


    Zooloretto
        The Familiarisation Area – The familiarisation area fits around the zoo board. It has space for 1 animal tile that can be put there for free. This tile doesn't score any points at the end of the game, neither plus nor minus points. During the game this tile can be moved into the zoo for free or you can trade for an animal in another familiarisation area for 1 coin. So basically this is a temporary "storage" for animals.
        The Petting Zoo – Each player receives a petting zoo and can place up to two baby animals in this space, receiving one coin for the first animal and two for the second. These animals cannot be moved and generate no points.
    



    Aquaretto
        The Trains – Eighteen train tiles, showing trains in one of four colors or a curved track, are mixed with the other tiles. When you take a train tile, you place it in an empty space in your zoo. Each subsequent train tile must connect to existing ones. Each type of train tile is worth two points at game's end.
        Three Coworker Tiles – These tiles are placed in the center of the table and can be claimed when a player gets coworkers. Each tile is different and cannot be used by other players once claimed.
    



    Either game
        The Savings Book – Each player receives a savings book that depicts the numbers 1-4. Instead of a normal action, the player can place a coin on 1 in his savings book. At the end of a round, a coin in the savings book advances one space, returning to the player – along with an extra coin – when it reaches 4.

The goal of the game is to create a natural legacy free from pollution. Game board is a like in Monopoly. Each player has a player board with 6 boxes: park, monument, beach, river, animal and plant. Player try to bring the corresponding 6 cards in correct places.

In his turn, the player rolls the dice and moves his pawn accordingly. In certain boxes, the player draws cards with pollution, cleanup, events etc.

The winner is the one who completes his six cards by being rid of all chips "pollution".

Be the first player to “go Rack-O” on each hand by drawing and discarding until you have ten cards aligned in numerical order -- either high to low OR low to high.

Deal cards to each player and they keep them in the order they were dealt but place them in a row face-up.  The dealer will take the top card off the draw pile and trades it with any card in their sequence, passing the removed card to the next player for them to use.  Players MUST swap the passed card with a card in their sequence.  Once the card goes around the table and reaches the dealer, it is discarded and it's the next player's turn to draw a new card.  Special Wild and Swap cards are also in the deck.

Who? Where? What?
Trivia game of Olympics 1896-2012.
There are 400 cards in this game. There is question and 4 variants in one side of the card and answer in another side.
Players gives their answer and if this correct player scores a point.
Player with the most points at the end of the game is the winner.

Round pins in a variety of colors with the silhouette of the Munchkin Man.

Special Rule: Each Munchkin Pin you are wearing gives you a +1 bonus in combat. After each fight you are in, win or lose, roll a d6 – if you roll equal to or less than the number of Munchkin Pins you are currently wearing, remove one pin. You may not wear more than one pin of each color during any game.

There are six colors:

     Tan with brown Munchkin Man
     Black with red Munchkin Man
     Maroon with yellow Munchkin Man
     Black with blue Munchkin Man
     Blue with yellow Munchkin Man
     Yellow with orange Munchkin Man


Part of the Munchkin series.

A special collection of games, all using the same tiles consisting of 1-6 squares showing 1 to 6 flowers. In all of these games these tiles must be placed in a frame; the way these are placed is different in the games. All these games help logic and spatial thinking, math, shape and color recognition.
Some examples:
"Lucky gardener": roll a die. Take one, two or three of your tiles. Use basic math (+, -, x, /) to reach the number rolled using the three numbers of the flowers on the individual tiles (e.g. your tiles have 1 flower, 2 flowers. 3 flowers and you roll a 4: 2+3-1=4) and you can place all the tiles used in the frame. The first player to fill the frame or place all his tiles wins.
"Clever Gardener": when it's your turn you try to make the garden have one or two more flowers than before. For this you can add and take away tiles, but only three tiles can be manipulated this way.

SETUP: Place the Distant Quarry in front of one of the light-brown Quarries, then place one sled on the new Quarry space on the tile.  At this point, place one Base Pyramid Stone on each of the sleds.

Turn order bonus Quarry move: You may alternately take a bonus Quarry Track Advance.

MOVE ACTIONS: In order to get on any sled on the Distant Quarry tile, you must move your workers down the quarry track with the Quarry Track advance option.

Quarry Track Advance: You may move ALL of your workers in one space down the quarry track together.

Return the sled and select new stone: When a sled in this row leaves the Quarry track it is returned to the Distant Quarry in the advanced space.

Was available at Essen 2012: "Game Variants & Mini-Expansions Set #2

Agricola: Pi-Deck adds 24 new cards—12 Minor Improvements and 12 Occupations—to Agricola.

This card set was designed and tested by users at Play-Agricola.com as part of the website's second large Agricola expansion, which consists of 139 cards (and is nicknamed G2). These 24 cards (nicknamed G2x) are the most unconventional of the group. The other 115 cards were released earlier in 2011 as the WM-Deck.

Gather stars from the cosmos to form constellations and rule the universe!
 THE CREATION
Divide the deck into constellation and star cards.  Shuffle the two decks and place them face-down next to  each other.  Each player draws three cards to begin their hand.  Turn two constellation cards face-up, side by side, next to the constellation draw pile.  A star discard pile will begin next to the star draw pile.  If the star draw pile is empty, reshuffle the discard pile and replace face-down.

HEAVENLY MOTION
During your turn:
1. Draw one star 
2. Do one of the following two actions:
a) Play two stars from your hand face-down on the table.  Your 
opponent(s) must place a star face-up in front of each of your two cards.  Your opponent wins the stars that were matched opposite the players star.  The player wins any unmatched cards opposite one another.
or
b) Discard  a set of star cards from your hand that match the stars highlighted on a face-up constellation card.  Take the constellation and earn the points labeled in roman numerals. Turn another constellation card face-up to replace the card just won.
If any player has less than two stars at the beginning of a turn, then, they draw to bring their hand to two cards.

STARS OF THE SHOW
Stars come in 4 types
alpha, beta, gamma, delta
9 cards of each type
Supernovae can be used as any star type
They always lose during a match phase
3 supernovae
15 Constellations in 3 types
3, 4, and 5 star combinations

2 players:
During a match phase, each player plays 2 stars
3 players:
During a match phase, the player plays 2 stars and each opponent plays 1 star
4 players:
Teams with 2 partners sitting opposite one another. 
Player and partner each play 1 star face-down
Opposing team’s partners each play 1 star

RULING THE UNIVERSE
The game ends when the last constellation has been taken.  Total the points in roman numerals that you or your team has collected.  Receive a 2 point bonus for collecting 3 or more of a constellation combination type. The highest total wins!

From the Rules:

"Let's help the Squinkies find their way back to the Squinkies Gumball Surprise Playhouse!  Be the first player to collect 4 different "Squinkies" cards; a friends, Babies, Puppies and Kitties card, and the So Cute card in hand.  Then return to the Gumball Surprise Pakyhouse and be the first player ready to play with yoru Squinkies!"

This is a basic children's roll-n-move game where you collect the cards by landing on the corresponding space.  Also, there is only ONE So Cute card.

Publisher Blurb:

Operations Vigilant Resolve and Phantom Fury - the operations to take and hold the city of Fallujah in the face of determined insurgent resistance - were amongst the most dangerous and controversial carried out by the Coalition forces during Operation Iraqi Freedom. These two battles of Fallujah saw some of the heaviest urban combat faced by regular troops in modern times and, even though much of the civilian populace had fled, the enemy was well-armed, well-prepared and driven by a fanatical resolve. With the scenarios and information presented in Fallujah, the latest Force on Force companion, wargamers will find themselves thrown into the narrow, twisting streets of the city, forced to negotiate IEDs, snipers, barricades, and a multinational insurgent force.

Publisher Description, from:

http://smokealondonpeculiar.co.uk/index.php/about/soho/what-is-soho/

Soho! is a game of skill and judgement for 2-6 players of all ages inspired by the two things for which this small, historic patch of London is famous around the globe: its pubs, and its one-way system.

Each player is the editor of a small literary magazine. Before the next issue can be printed, six pieces of rashly commissioned copy need to be retrieved from a somewhat motley bunch of recalcitrant writers:

Travel blogger and author of Leicester: City of Crisps Toby D’Azure.
Girl-about-town and sparkly-heeled chick-lit tyro Sophie Blush.
Postmodern goremeister and connoisseur of noir Justin Slick.
Aga-endorsing barbour-clad romantic novelist Lavinia Snowe.
Former Para turned lad-mag agony uncle David “Dave” Green.
Otherworldly and oddly androgynous sci-fi bod CT Vermillion.

All are published authors but, on account of not being published as often as they feel they should be, will still take freelance work wherever they can get it, and aren’t too fussed about style or topic: in fact, all insist that being able to turn in stuff “outside their normal comfort zone” stops them getting stuck in a generic rut and keeps them fresh and edgy; the alternative suggestion – that they have no shame and will do anything for a free lunch and a small sub to get them through till next Friday – is, they say, just insulting nonsense. On this, they are all agreed; on everything else, they are united only in their mutual contempt, jealousy, and suspicion. They are, in a word, writers.

And, being writers, all six are currently holed up in six Soho pubs, cadging free drinks, chatting up people half their age (but with, oddly, twice their looks), and complaining vociferously about their agents, about dumbing down in the publishing industry, and about how they didn’t want that Eastenders gig anyway as it would have compromised their artistic integrity and also possibly involved buying a TV licence.

The noble editors’ thankless task is to contact all six writers and extricate their beer/sauvignon-stained prose from whichever unwholesome pocket or handbag it’s been stuffed in. The first to do so scores a small moral victory or, to borrow a phrase from Monopoly, wins.

To get from one pub to the next the editors can walk, hail a cab, or grab a Boris Bike. Taxis are fast, but much of Soho is alleyways or paved, and it’s not easy finding somewhere to pull up. Bikes are nippy, but what about those cobbles on Broadwick Street? – ouch! And, of course, this is Soho, where the streets are narrow and the inexplicable commonplace ...

User summary
A 2-player assymetrical card game, where one player is a band of thieves trying to steal treasures, and the other player is the police force trying to protect them. The Thief has a deck of cards numbered 1–6 of different colours, and the Police has a couple of trap cards, and a large number of duds (incompetent police officers).

Seven treasures are lined up in a row, and the players take turns placing one card each. The thief plays his cards face up, and the Police face down. If the thief plays cards that apply to one of the traps the police has played, the trap is played face up and the polices trap and all of the thief's cards for that treasure are discarded.

If the Thief managed to steal 4 treasures, he wins. If the Police manages to protect 4 treasure, he wins.

The art is of several famous master thieves from Japanese TV animations.

This is vintage game of abstract naval strategy.

Four warships are attempting to destroy an enemy submarine. The board includes spaces with submarine nets and the warships attempt to force the sub into the entanglements. The sub can fire a torpedo at a warship by maneuvering into special "fire" spaces that permit the U-boat captain to launch.

The game can be played by more than two players... each additional player commands an individual warship or a squadron of ships in team play.

Includes the board and five metal ship tokens.

The Gazala Gallop is  an Operational level board-wargame set during World War 2 in North Africa.

The Basic Game - which is the active one - uses the popular 1st Alamein/Battles for Alamein wargame system.  It is regiment to division unit level, 5mis/8kms per hex, and has 14 2-day gameturns.  (The Advanced Game is still there for its map, for anyone curious.)

The Afrika Korps has superior speed/maneuverability, while the British 8th Army's 2nd and 22nd Armoured Brigades (which had the notoriously unreliable Crusader tanks) are subject to the British Breakdown Rule.  The American Lend-Lease Grant medium tanks are the backbone of the most powerful British unit, 7th Armoured Brigade, while Valentine and slow, plodding Matilda British infantry tanks are in the 1st and 32nd Tank Brigades.

Airpower is abstractly modeled, as in Battles for Alamein.

The Allies have the option of a non-historical deployment, but are penalized in Victory Points for utilizing it.

As historically, Allied units are slow to activate/respond, so the Axis Player must hit fast and hard to flank and/or penetrate the Allies' Gazala Line and finally take Fortress Tobruk, to win.

Rules are provided - subject to revision.

12Nov12:  I've broken the German Recce Gruppee down into the 3 divisional Recce bns - three 1-1*-5s instead of just the one 2-2*-5.

And KG Hecker is now a 2-2-4 ... although maybe that should have just been a 1-2-4.

description:
Arabian Sails is an expansion for Caravans of Asia, a trading game set on the Silk Road in the days of Marco Polo.

In this expansion, players take to the high seas, sailing with the monsoon winds of the Indian Ocean.  The board is expanded to include East Africa, the rest of India, and the islands of Southeast Asia.

Arabian Sails adds new board pieces, trade goods, event cards, ship tokens, and rules for sailing and winds.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
There's a huge floatin' shin-dig happenin' across the water. She's gonna be elbow to elbow over there so we need to round up a bunch o' boats, fill 'em with boozed-up locals, and get their asses over there before dock space is gone!

Lakebilly is a rude and crude party'n game that is easy to learn, very social, and hilarious. You play the captain - your job is to fill up as many boats as you can with boozy locals and deliver them to the lake party as fast as possible.

Stay sharp. Dock space is limited and all of the other captains on the water are rounding up their crew too. Make sure you bribe the locals with beers to get them aboard right away. Watch out for sabotage! The other captains will steal your boats, mutiny your crew and do anything to stop you from getting to the party location.
The promo-card features an additional vehicle, the Cthulhu in 2012 Campaign Bus. The base game can be played with one more player.

boardgame: Nuns on the Run: The Monk
description:
Father Robert (a visiting monk who happens to be deaf) begins the game in the Outer Garden by the Garden Entry (location 84).  The Guard player takes the 6 movement tokens.  The Guard player doesn't take a Blessing Card for the monk. One turn one, the guard selects one of the 3 garden gate paths to have the monk follow: either to the Pharmacy (122), the Kitchen (147) or the Scriptorium (101).  The monk moves during the Guards Move phase of the game, and only walks (3 or 4 spaces).

Since the monk is deaf, he does not get a sound check, nor do novices need to check to see if he hears them during the novice's noise check.  He does, however see just like the other guard characters do (180-degree vision, up to 6 spots away).  If a novice enters his field of vision, they must reveal themselves. The monk cannot capture a novice, and novices may move through his position without penalty.  If a novice shares the same space as the monk the novice is detected. When the monk arrives at his path destination, choose another token that includes his current location and continue his movements.

You will only use 4 of the 6 tokens during the game - 2 of the 3 garden gate tokens (entering and leaving the convent) and 2 of the 3 tokens that allow travel between the Pharmacy, Kitchen, and Scriptorium.

The guard player may use any combination of tokens, but you may visit a convent location only once (with the exception of the Garden Gate).  Once the monk returns to the Garden Gate, remove him from the game.

Legacy is a competitive puzzle game for any number of players. Using a random deck of cards numbered 1-90 plus three special "journey" cards, players attempt to achieve the highest total score from three unique elements/configurations -- Opus, Garden, and Empire -- before the last journey card is drawn. The elements have unique placement rules and scoring formulas which will generate agonizing decisions throughout the game. The "board" comprises of a full sheet of paper where the players will record the drawn card values into their individual elements.

A game that owes something to Solitaire. The board is an equitateral triangle with parallel lines connecting each of the 55 holes.

Three game versions are included. The first is for two or three players and starts with the board emptied of all it's pegs. Each player takes turns in adding a peg. If the peg completes a line, the player scores points. When the board is filled up, the winner is the player with the most points.

Game two starts with the board filled with pegs except the the hole in the middle of row seven. Each player takes turns to jump over a peg Solitaire-style and can continue jumping if there are more jumps available to him. The winner is the one with the most pegs once no more jumps are possible.

The third game is for one player who starts with the board full of pegs except for the one in the middle of row seven. The game is won if the player can take off every peg by jumping them and get down to just one peg left. For a harder game, that last peg must be in the hole which was vacant at the start (the one in the middle of row seven).

With the crack of her rifle and the smell of gunpowder, One Shot brings her signature ranged warfare into Super Dungeon Explore with deadly effect.  Terrified monsters flee in panic, but they can't run fast enough.  One Shot has them in her sights!

Contents:
One Shot Hero Model
25mm Base

Kasaro To has no love for heroics.  Lord Nozuki has corrupted the Super Dungeon Explore code, allowing his most favored servant to invade the Consul's dungeons.  Kasaro To is prepared to wreak carnage and destruction upon the hapless heroes of Crystalia.

Contents:

Kisa is every bit at home in the world of Super Dungeon Explore as she is in the Last Galaxy.  Steeped in arcane lore and magical might, Kisa blasts and curses the dungeon's monsters with glee and style.

Contents:
Kisa Hero Model
25mm Base

Few would suspect the dour Sebastian Cross to be an avid Super Dungeon Explore player.  Yet he is renown for his cunning tactics and exceptional party management.  A party led by Sebastian is an unstoppable juggernaut, battering through the Consul's minions with grim determination.

Contains:
Sebastian Cross Hero Model
25mm Base
Game Card

Players are elfs who are seeking for gold while crossing a giant Rainbow. Running fast may guarantee them to be the first to arrive on the Pot of Gold at the other side. The player who collects more gold coins or the one who arrives first at the Pot wins. It's a game of pushing your luck and trying to trick your opponents. In each move players will try to guess if the next card is higher or lower than the previous one, to advance one step. Some lightenings are played against the others to try to fool them and make them return to the beginning while clowds are used as a protection. A player collects as many gold coins as the cards of the same color correctly revealed, when he decides to stop. Some simple tactics and a lot of interaction awaits you in this Pot-o-Gold Run!

In October 2012 the game was finalist in the Boardgame Design Contest of Panzazol Joue (France).

This is trick-taking game. This game was printed in Canada and were sold only in Korea.

Round number cards each round, each player receives. Each player takes a trick to obtain their expected those numbers and call records. And play the game and get the score accordingly.

This is a trick-taking game that is very similar to Wizard.

NAVIGATOR
Set Your Goal...
...Navigate To It

boardgame: A game where an enclosed short young lady, a perfect class representative, a slightly elder classy maid, a normal high school girl with the aura of a hamster, among others, try to win an unlucky-looking girl-faced butler with a debt ... [truncated]
description:
１億６千万円ぐらいの借金をかかえた不幸そうな女顔の執事を、ひきこもりぎみのチビなお嬢様や、完璧超人の生徒会長やら、年上の優秀なメイドさん、ハムスターなオーラーの普通の女子高生たちで奪い合うゲーム。女装もあるよ ("A game where an enclosed short young lady, a perfect class representative, a slightly elder classy maid, a normal high school girl with the aura of a hamster, among others, try to win an unlucky-looking girl-faced butler with a debt of about 160 million yen. There's cross-dressing, too")  is a simple "pull the joker" style card game.

A card deck consisting of some seven or eight different types of cards. There is an even number of cards except for the "butler" card. All cards are dealt, and players take turns drawing one card at random from the previous player. If any pair is formed, it's discarded and the special effect of that card (if any) happens. If a player runs out of cards, he's out. The player with the last "butler" card left in hand wins.

—user summary

Note: The title is actually a bit longer than the BGG entry allows. It should end with "debt of about 160 million yen. There's crossdressing, too" but it is of minor importance.

description:
These are difficult times: the war is imminent in ungoverned lands.Around the remains of an ancient Celtic settlement, a group of thugs, hastily, built a fortress to protect themselves from the enemies who pursued them and to begin a new life.
You will need to collaborate in the construction of the fortress. Your effort will be rewarded with the leadership of the square if your men and women working hard against those thugs.

The player in turn makes one or two actions possible, using two cards from your hand.Then fill your hand with two new cards from the deck and ends their turn.

Actions:
-Obtain resources
-Develop tiles
-Build a Wall
-Build a Door
-Build a Tower
-Build a Guild
-Place fundraiser
-Retreat to the temple

Winner of the 1st contest of "Ojo al dado" (Eye to die) of creation of boardgames ( 23th february 2013)link

SETUP: Take a subsidy contract tile.

RENEW: You may use your subsidy contract tile to add one worker to the total number of workers committed to renewing a building.  This is a rainbow colored worker and can be used as a worker of any color.  If used for a worker then you must discard this tile.

HIRE: You may use your subsidy contract tile to add one coin to the total number of coins needed to hire a specialist.  This is a rainbow colored coin and can be used as a coin of any color.  If used for a coin then you must discard this tile.

ITALIAN POLITICS VARIANT: The subsidy contract tile has 2 sides, active and inactive.  It begins the game with its active side up.  When you use it flip it over to its inactive side.  For the rest of the game, if you lose control of a specialist (someone buys it from you), flip the subsidy contract tile back to its active side.

SETUP: After you create the face up cargo draft pile, place the Space Port tile next to the maneuver deck on the opposite side and turn over the top maneuver card in the deck and place it on the Space Port tile.

FUELING: Whenever you draw a card, you may exchange one card from your hand with the maneuver card in the Space Port.  You may have a maximum hand size of 6.

MID-GAME SCORING VARIANT: Place a marker in front of the first player.  When the mid-game scoring is triggered, each player is given the opportunity of an equal number of turns.  New cards are not replenished in the planetary display, but each player is allowed to continue to make any available deliveries until the player to the left of the starting player finishes their turn.  Now continue with the mid-game scoring as normal.

The players must solve a series of riddles arranged on individual cards. 
The youngest player starts, one of the players took one of the cards, show it to the player in turn and immediately turn the sandglass.
If the player solve the riddle before time has expired shall place the card on his personal board, if the player do not solve correctly the card is placed in the bottom of the deck.
The game ends when one of the players solve six riddles. 
Simple and easy.

It's a family game containing 90 cards, 1 die and a few tokens.

A central board is built with one type of cards (table-cards). Each player receives 7 cards from the pile (game-cards).

Players have to collect the 7 colors of the Rainbow as soon as possible in order to score more points. The mechanic is quite simple: 1. draw a card and reveal it (use the bonus action if a bonus card was drawn); 2. Exchange a card of your hand with another one on the board (carefull: some cards are blocked and cannot be exchanged unless you use the die). After a player turn the others may struggle to exchange for free (no turn action) the card left by the previous player.

The 1st player collecting the 7 different colors wins the maximum points (depending on the number of players). Then, by finishing order, others gain less 1 point than the previous one. Scoring: each color card and each pot-o-gold worth 1 point. Add the scoring tokens and know your scoring.

It is Spring 1940, and both the British and the Germans have plans to invade neutral Norway.  The British still have the French on their side, while the German Kriegsmarine will never be larger.  Who will strike first?

Norway 1940 is a ziplock expansion for PQ-17 that adds all the British, French, Polish, and German naval and air units needed to re-fight this tense air-naval battle, including such seldom-seen ships and aircraft as the repair ship HMS Vindictive, German Type II U-boats, Blackburn Skuas and Rocs, and early Ju88 heavy fighters.  Special rules reflect the unique conditions of the campaign, such as Scandinavian neutrality, Norwegian coastal defenses, faulty German torpedoes, shore bombardments, and Allied establishment of a forward naval base (historically in the Lofotens).  And just as in the parent game, secretly determined special conditions offer new opportunities and threats to both sides.  Will the British pre-empt the German invasion?  Will the Germans attempt to break out into the Atlantic with their heavy ships?  Will the Allies elect to stay in Narvik instead of evacuating with the fall of France imminent?  Or will the Germans make a daring attempt to seize Iceland before the Allies can occupy it in force?

Norway 1940 includes three operational scenarios highlighting key periods of the campaign.  Operation WESERÜBUNG covers early April 1940, with both sides attempting to land ground forces in neutral Norway.  Operation SICKLE covers the second half of the month, as the Allies counter-attack after the successful German invasion.  And Operation JUNO depicts the end of the campaign in early June, when the Germans attempt to interfere with the Allied offensive on Narvik, unaware that the Allies have already decided to evacuate (or have they?).  Plus a 1940 Campaign scenario allows players to refight the entire 10-week campaign, including a semi-abstract system for ground operations that will ultimately decide victory in the game.

Components:

    115 1" square counters 
    140 1/2" square markers 
    26 circular counters in 3 sizes 
    Two 8-1/2 x 11" Player Aid Cards 
    One 8-1/2 x 11" Campaign Display 
    Two 8-1/2 x 5-1/2" Port Displays 
    2nd Edition PQ-17 Rules booklet 
    Play booklet

boardgame: Panzer: Game Expansion Set, Nr 3 – Drive to the Rhine: The Second Front 1944-45
description:
This 3rd Panzer expansion set includes the most significant US and British forces that saw action on the Western front in 1944 up until the end of the war in 1945. Also included are the German units that fought against their relentless advances from Normandy, across Italy, breaching the Siegfried Line, and finally pushing in to the German Fatherland.

The playbook for this third expansion includes rules for special units, new optional rules, Unit summaries for the US, British and German units and 10 scenarios. The list of Scenarios can be found below in the Community Wiki section.

The TO&Es booklet includes details for the US, British and German forces covering the period from 1944 through 1945, including the US eavy and light Armored Divisions, British Armoured Divisions, in both Western Europe and Italian configurations, and Infantry and Airborne Divisions. German forces include Panzer Lehr, the 101st SS Heavy Tank Battalion (Michael Wittmann commanded its second company) and two independent Panzer Brigades.

Finally, this expansion includes the next 4 double-sided geomorphic mapboards (8 mapboards total), modeling the terrain found in the close confines of the French boscage country and many other features as the Allied forces push into the heart of Germany. Add these 4 geomorphic mapboards to the 6 geomorphic mapboards from Expansions 1 & 2 for added play and many possible battle combinations.

In order to play this expansion only the Panzer base game is required. Ownership of Expansions 1 & 2 is not required to field the German forces, they are all included here. However, this expands on those German units included in the first two expansions, no unit IDs are duplicated. Combine the German units from all three expansions to create customized scenarios on either the Western or Eastern Front.

Included Panzer Exp3 units:

     Each symbol is shown exactly 9 times.
     Each domino is shown exactly 3 times.
     There are exactly 72 squares; the same number of squares that compose the 36 tiles.


The first board configuration with the above properties was found by Mark Tilford.

“Shinsen-gumi” covers the actions of shinsen-gumi in Kyoto city during its high time.
Each player acts as one of its team leaders, such as Saitoh, Nagakura, Harada.
Players must manage his team members and their armory to keep the security of Kyoto.
The enemies appear by random card and players must fight with them.  Some important historical events such as Ikedaya-incident, duel of Abura-kohji could happen during the game.
The key member of Shinsen-gumi, such as Hijikata, Okita could reinforce players team randomly.
The beautiful map covers from Imadegawa street to 7-jou street and from Kamo river to Mibu-temple.
1 map, 2 counter sheets, 72 cards

Some cards have special abilities, and players also have a special character ability.

The winner gets the right to marry Marisa.

description:
Attrition is designed to be played by two people. Players follow directions provided on the game board to form completed boxes and capture the most amount space.

The board is 13x13 one inch squares (totaling 169 spaces)each with its own arrows allowing for movement of a specific direction up, down, left or right, or a combination of those movements.

At the beginning of the game each player rolls a die to determine who will place their game piece first (lower number places first higher number places second but moves first). As players exit a tile they place a marker turned to their color on the board. Players must follow the directional arrows in the spaces trying to form a completed box (2x2 or greater). Once you have formed a completed box you are allowed to move freely in that space assuming you can get to the box using directional arrows on the board. If a completed box is fully encircled by the opponents color markers the box is turned to that players markers. You may not move through an opponents completed box. If you move through an opponents marker that is not in a completed box turn it to your color (you must follow the directions of the arrows under the marker). If at any time you find that you have no legal moves you move your game piece to another location on the board and your remaining moves are forfeit.

Each round players roll a single die and the combined roll is the movement allotment for the turn.  Players alternate first position each round eliminating the "first-player advantage".

The game ends when neither player has a valid move that they can take.
  
The aesthetics of the game are meant to give you that old-timey feel. From the scroll for a game mat to the stained wooden game pieces and markers, Attrition is meant to feel like a game that has been played for hundreds of years.

This expansion includes all the additional boards that have been or will be released for the original game Heptalion.

All the boards share the same constraints:

a) Each symbol is shown exactly 8 times.
b) Each domino is shown exactly 3 times.
c) There are exactly 56 squares; the same number of squares that compose the 28 tiles.

The first distribution (the one used in the base game) was found by Néstor Romeral Andrés on Jun-21-2012 and has the shape of a diamond.

The second distribution was found by Grant Fikes on Sep-18-2012 and it's rounded.

The third distribution is a 'n' and was found by Mark Tilford.

Carcassonne: Little Buildings is a promotional expansion released with SpielBox magazine issue 5/2012.  The expansion consists of 18 building tokens: 6 houses, 6 sheds, and 6 towers. Each player gets an equal number of these tokens at the beginning of the game to place on a tile in place of a meeple.  When a road, city, cloister, or field is scored, it receives 1 additional point for each tile that is shared with a "Little Building". A variant is included where the sheds/houses/towers are treated differently and score 1/2/3 additional point(s) when the structures are completed. Part of the Carcassonne series.

Overview:
City of the Century is a heavy stock/economic game based in Chicago during the Industrial Age.

Players gain points from playing stock cards into their tableau. The stock cards correspond to four different industries: Manufacturing, Railroads, Livestock, and Grain.  These industries increase in profit through the course of the game by the players collectively operating Chicago. Each time a player increases an industry's profit, they can either draw an stock card or play an stock card from their hand.

Gameplay:
Each turn, players can perform two of the available actions: Ship, Manufacture, Build, Run Chicago, and Run the Canal. Through these actions, players manipulate the six major goods that were vital to Chicago becoming the heart of the Industrial Revolution: Steel, Coal, Livestock, Grain, Lumber, and Brick.

Winning:
Players win by totaling the points scored from their stock cards, buildings they constructed, and other small bonuses that appear throughout the game.

The biggest vault in town is empty! Was it Gloomy Gary, Burgling Beth or Wicked Wally? The players investigate who left the fingerprints on the crime site and who has an alibi. Only the player who can quickly combine numbers, identify the right suspect and, with the right operation card, take him into custody will win the game as the best 
crime solver.

In the quick reaction game Nabbed!, twelve criminal suspects are arranged in a circle, with a police officer placed next to one of the suspects. Each player receives operation cards numbered 1-6.

Each turn, someone reveals a profile card that shows three suspects, then another player rolls the die. The symbol on the die tells the players which of the three suspects on the profile card is the criminal. Players race to count the number of steps the officer must go to reach the criminal, then reveal the corresponding operation card from their hand. The player who reveals the card first checks whether the answer is correct by moving the officer the indicated number of spaces. If he was right, he collects the profile card; otherwise all other players receive a profile card. Whoever collects six profile cards first wins!

User summary

A memory-styled yet complex card game based on the Japanese TV animation series Puella Magi Madoka Magica. The game consists of 6 witch cards, 16 Puella Magi cards, 10 emotion cards, and some fate (point) cards.

The 16 puella magi cards are placed face down in a grid, and the goal of the game is to – like Memory – on your turn, reveal the correct puella magi in order to defeat the current witch. Defeating the witches gains the player a fate card and an emotion cards. Emotion cards can be used for special effects. Also, each player has a secret identity that gets certain bonus points at the end, but it's dangerous if the other players discover who you are.

In La Chasse aux Lutins, many cute elves are hidden in your house. The player needs to find where they are hidden and catch them before anyone else can, and whoever collects more elves on his cards wins.

How do the elves hide in your house? They get a little help from the players. In the basic game, the cards have one, two or three elves on them, and these cards are split evenly among the players. The players then go into different rooms in the house and hide the cards, with the one elf cards being fully visible, the two elf cards being half hidden, and the three elf cards being completely hidden. Players then start from a central location and hunt for cards, staying away from the room in which they hid cards since they already know where to find those elves!

La Chasse aux Lutins includes a variant that uses cards with mischievous elves. These are hidden along with the other elf cards, and if a player finds at least three of these cards, he scores an additional three points that's added to his elf score. If he finds only one or two of these cards, however, he scores no bonus and must throw away one of the elf cards he collected!

A long, long time ago, after a loot of a cursed treasure, a pirate ship was devastated by an intense storm and pushed into the lowlands of a distant archipelago. The enigmatic medallions recovered by the natives over the years and the strange events that have taken place ever since, make the population on the islands fear that the curse is upon them. In Medallions, players embrace the characters of the natives charged with exorcising the curse and seeking to recover the medallions to launch them into a giant furnace. The purpose is to transform them into pure metal ingots. But beware: not all members on the mission are well-intentioned...

In Medallions players have to collect medallions of 3 colors within the islands to transform them into gold ingots (VP's). In their turn players move their disc on the track up to 3 cases. Then, their pawn inside the board repeats the move selected. To continue the movement and go further a player may use cards from a common stack. At the beginning of the game each player receives a secret role (there may be a traitor or not in the team). If you are a good charater you will try to transform the highest number of medallions you can, trying to identify and accuse an eventual traitor (there are risks doing so). The traitor will have another goal: collect secretly the highest number of black medallions (traitor VP's). A traitor only can be accused until 3/4 of the game track.

The game has a simple but important economic gestion. To have money to buy new medallions arriving in a island, players will need to sell medallions and special ability cards that may be played in your turn. As they are sold, the pile will increase and someone can buy them all! Note that sold medallions are put face down, and only buyers can have access to the information about who sold what.

Kensei is a Samurai Feudal Medieval Miniature Tabletop Wargame where players take control of one of the 3 factions; Kuge (Court Nobility), Sohei (Warrior Monks) and Buke (Warriors) fighting for supremacy over feudal Japan and for the honor of the Emperor of Himukai.

The miniatures are 28 mm and require assemble and painting, requiring some modeling skills.

As a wargame, it requires a rulebook, 10-sided dice, scenery, a tape measure, and some tokens as a reminder of actions.

A fast-paced and hilarious card game of bluff and sabotage.

The goal of the game is to be the player holding onto the Package card when the end game condition is triggered, which is the draw deck running out. Players start with 5 cards. Every turn, a player either draws a card or plays one from his/her card. Playing a card may allow the player to draw additional cards, steal cards from another player or exchange cards with another player. There is a hand limit of 7 cards.

Captain Peg-leg and his crew have captured a load of booty. Now it's time to distribute it, piece by piece. But watch out that nobody makes a mistake! Otherwise parrot Koko will raise the alarm and reckless mutiny will ensue. Which pirate with good memory skills will be the fastest at snatching and win the most treasure pieces?

Reckless Mutiny is a memory game about pirates dividing their booty according to the old pirate law. Players take turns drawing a booty card, showing it to all other players, then placing it face down in front of a pirate – but no pirate may get two cards in a row with the same kind of booty or with booty of the same color, so you must always remember the last card placed in front of each pirate.

If a player thinks a booty card was misplaced, he grabs the parrot and shouts "Reckless Mutiny!" Players look at their booty cards, and if the card was placed incorrectly, the player who grabbed the parrot gets the booty card. If the card was placed correctly, the player who placed that card – that is, the one who was wrongfully accused – gets the card instead. Play continues until all 56 booty cards have been distributed, and whoever acquires the most cards wins.

Reckless Mutiny includes variants for more experienced players. One variant use the money cards in the booty deck, with the captains needing to place all of these cards in numerical order. Thus, if a money card is drawn, the player must recall all the other money cards already played and place that card in the right position. In another variant you flip a coin to determine whether you place booty cards as in the basic game or whether you place them on a card that shares at least one common characteristic (color or kind of booty).

Trivitria Up combines trivia and tactics in a unique way. Players try to earn and get their own building pieces to the uprising tower while avoiding making the tower collapse. 
Players can get their building pieces to the tower by succeeding with the trivia questions, with a skillful roll of a dice or if other players don't get their answers right. All the players build the same tower and exciting tactics comes to play when players try to make tower building hard for others.
Goal in the game is to get as many own building pieces to the uprising tower as possible. Winner is the player who had most building pieces in the tower at the time of tower collapse. Loser is... well, do we have to say it..

description: Blockade Runner: Advanced Action Cards, a home-brewed and authorized expansion for Blockade Runner, allows you to hamper and delay your opponents, while you get the chance to buy goods for cheaper prices or sell them in the best conditions. The law against unfair competition has apparently not been invented yet...

The game consists of a board and twelve dice, three for each of the four players. Each players starts at one side of the board with a die numbered 1, one numbered 3 and one numbered 5. These numbers specify the distance a die can move over the board. The goal is to get three dice in a row. Dice cannot jump over other dice, so the players try to block each other. Instead of a move, a player can turn a die to any number. The central field of the board cannot be occupied, and the eight fields around it double the score when a player gets his/her row done on them.

Alea was published in 1980 in Göttingen as part of Dieter Hartmann's self publishing line.

Fun survival board game that is set in a cartoon-like environment. The game board is compiled from 37 individual tiles, which are placed randomly and face down. The turn system is fast paced. During your turn you can carry out the following actions in any order:
A. Move to any adjacent tile (1x). If hidden, the tile is revealed instantly.
B. Pick up a new card from the deck corresponding to your tile (1x).
C. Use cards in combinations or on their own (unlimited times).

There are 200 game cards divided in 5 tile types (40 each). Each zone has different possibilities of cards to find. There are 23 different game cards. Some of these cards can be used on their own, but others only in 10 specific combinations.

The game has different rule variations and the main game modes have somewhat opposite goals, which are: 
1) Survivor - the goal is to be the least hungriest when someone loses.
2) Last Survivor - the goal is to stay in the game for as long as possible. So it is played till everyone but one loses.
3) Forage - the goal is to be the first to reaches 20 forage points.

During the game-play you can place traps to protect your territory against other players or to hunt (each deck has 6 wild boar cards - which start their movement on the board). Also each deck has 3 Depleted Tile cards - which means that you cannot stay in one place all the time. The game board will get more and more depleted as the game advances. It's also possible to play long enough to have all the tiles depleted. At this point you still carry out your turns as you normally would, only there are no cards to pick up so you have to play with what you got left in your hand.

The expected gaming session highly varies depending on the player count, their luck/skill level and the game mode you have chosen to play with maybe including also some optional rules.
For 2 players the Survivor mode can take as long as 2 hours to play out while the Forage mode can take as fast as 30 minutes. With 5+ players it's quite the opposite - the more players you got the faster the game will end in Survivor mode, while in Forage it will go on much longer.

Tetragon2 is an abstract strategy game for 2 that can play very quick, or take longer, (15 mn to an hour") depending on the match up. Tetragon2 is a game of creation, and the first player to create two tetragons wins...possibly, the other player gets one more move!

The play pieces are triangle in shape, made of maple and walnut, real hard wood! Each player has 9 wood triangles, there is no capturing and no jumping, the pieces move in a variety of ways, forward one square, sideways one square, backwards one square, or diagonal as far as you can go without pivoting or hitting another piece. Tetragons in the center four squares are 10 points, and  anywhere else they are 5 points.

A simple, roll and move game with the object being to be the first player to acquire 5000 votes. Like the Bowes radio show, where if he did not like your act he rang a loud GONG signifying the end of your radio audition, the game has a couple of GONG spaces, where landing on them returns you to the beginning of the game (unlike the radio show where being gonged meant the end of your performance). Other spaces can make you go back some number of spaces. or move ahead some number of spaces.

The game (Popular Edition 1) is based on the MAJOR BOWES' Original Amateur hour on radio (the predecessor to the Ted Mack Amateur hour on TV in the early 1950's). Major Bowes who never was in the military insisted on being addressed by his self-proclaimed title of "MAJOR" also had a saying that he was famous for. He always said "ALL RIGHT! ALL RIGHT!" after each performer had either finished his/her act or been gonged off the show.

Famous words associated with the show and printed on the game board:

"THE WHEEL OF FORTUNE SPINS AND WHERE SHE STOPS NOBODY KNOWS"


PUBLISHER: WARNER MFG CO, BENNINGTON, VT

boardgame: Rainbow Fish Look Out For the Shark!
description:
The Rainbow Fish Look Out For the Shark game is ideal for 3-5 players. The object of the game is to get rid of all of you card-and avoid ending up with the shark card.

The dealer shuffles the cards and deals them all, face down to all the players. Players shoudl be careful not to show the faces of their cards to the other players. Any player who holds a pair of card with the same symbols (green leaf, red starfish, yellow snail, etc) in the top left corners should immediately lay them face up on the table. Player to the left of the dealer goes first. He or she picks a card from the player to their left. If the card matches, the player lays them down face up on the table. If the card does not match, or gets the dreaded shark card, their turn ends and it is the next players turn. Play continues until all pairs have been matched up and one player is left holding the shark card. The player with the shark is the looser. This game features the art of Marcus Pfister's award winning book series The Rainbow Fish.

boardgame: Great Art Close up: Museum of Fine Arts, Boston
description:
Great Art Close Up...

Discover art of the great museums of the world as you play 5 fun card games, including "Memory," "Old Maid," and 2 new matching games for children of different ages. Turn your next visit to the art museum into a treasure hunt with each Close Up deck.

Paintings from this game are from Museum of Fine Arts, Boston

Look closely to match close up details to the paintings they come from with this deck of 48 playing cards. Ages 5 to adult.
family: Series: Great Art Close Up (Birdcage Press)
Theme: Fine Art and Art Museums

description: Bird watching is America’s most popular pastime. This card game + book set features some of North America’s most fascinating birds, from owls and raptors to waders and woodland birds. Learn about birds as you play three favorite card games: Memory, Old Maid, and Go Fish. Created in association with the Cornell Lab of Ornithology, and filled with wonderful images from leading wildlife photographers.

boardgame: Solidarity: El juego de la ayuda humanitaria
description:
Solidarity: El juego de la ayuda humanitaria

Educational game. The players are in the role of ONGD's that try to help with projects around the World to erradicate the poverty and help development in the Third World.

In the process, they also have to answer questions about  Geography, international solidarity, discoveries and miscelanea.

Game mechanics: A roll-and-move trivia game. The players roll a die and move around the mapboard, having to answer questions, confront unexpected events and getting the necessary money during the process to do the projects. The first player to complete a determined number of projects wins the game.

Version for children of classic Czech pre-revolution monopoly clone about horses.

Buy horse champions and place them in your stable. Earn money when other players visit your stable, lose money when you visit theirs. Hire trainers, draw cards of luck and finances, place bets on horses. Last player not bankrupt wins the game.
One of few "modern" board games available before 1989 revolution in former Czechoslovakia. Along with Sorry!, Stratego and Scotland Yard clones a typical household feature.


Dětská varianta nejúspěšnější české hry Dostihy a sázky s přizpůsobenými pravidly pro děti. Hráči nakupují své poníky a další potřebnou koňskou výbavu. Během hry se nenásilně seznámí s hodnotou peněz a hlavně se dobře pobaví. Děti od 5 let mohou hrát zjednodušenou variantu bez peněz.
Balení obsahuje:
1 herní plán
12 karet poníků
12 kartónových figurek poníků
12 plastových stojánků
17 karet náhody
162 dostihových peněz
24 žetonů s ohlávkou a sedlem
4 figurky ve čtyřech barvách
1 bodová kostka
1 návod ke hře
family: Versions & Editions: Junior Versions of Grown-Up Games

description:
The Very Bad Game is a board game based on the wacky characters and themes of the immensely popular Bad Book series by Andy Griffiths - one of Australia's most successful writers.

How to play:
"For ages 7 and up, the game is easy to play and always fun. Players race around Badtown trying to find the 4 missing pieces of their lost pet. But it's not easy! They have to get past the weird and wacky folk of Badtown - who will send them every which way around the board for all kinds of very bad reasons. And watch out - the whole town may even blow up! It's a lot of nonsense and riotous family fun!"

Three dice are used - The Bad Die, The Very Bad Die and The Highway Die (which is used to cross the highways).

The game is expected to be the must-have companion to the Bad Book Series and the perfect gift for the hundreds of thousands of Griffith's fans. Developed in partnership with Andy Griffiths, The Very Bad Game is a genuine extension to the books - including 200 hilarious Very Bad Cards.
 It is cetain to bring hours of family fun with excellent game play, laughter, the enjoyment of reading and a little strategy. It is also a visual treat! Just like the books, the game includes hundreds of illustrations and a stunning game board by Terry Denton that bring the world of Badtown to life as only Terry can.
(Source: Relatively Creative website)

Players roll the dice and place correct blocks of ice to build the igloo. But beware, if the dice shows "Sneezy" bear comes out of his igloo and ejects blocks. The winner is the one who gets rid of his blocks.

This is a party game designed to be enjoyed by a family during Thanksgiving dinner.

Each player is provided with "Thanksgiving Cards" and writes down what he or she is thankful for this year. Examples include a promotion at the office, a new house, or the USA! A moderator chosen from among the contestants reads each response and the other players attempt to guess who filled out that card. The player with the highest number of correct guesses is declared the winner.

Junk is a card and dice game for 2-4 players that takes 45-90 minutes to play: instead of building civilization like you might be used to in the genre, in Junk you are REbuilding using the junk from the world before.

The players are leaders of a tribe that has survived an apocalypse and scavenge the wasteland for junk. These junk cards are drawn, traded, or stolen from other players to combine into pre-collapse technologies. The first tribe to restore civilization by achieving all 5 of these: the wonders of Sanitation, Preservation, Constitution, Radio, and Power Generation, wins.

How will you choose to survive? By nurturing the wasteland ecology and harvesting its resources, founding a warrior culture to take what others have, restoring culture and quality of life, studying the science of what came before, or learning how it all fit together?
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: The Story of Struggle Chaos in Gensokyo
description:
User summary

A deduction-style party game for 3–8 players based on characters and situations from Shrine Maiden fame. Based on Shadow Hunters
Players are all dealt characters with different set goals, and try to accomplish these goals during the game. Part of the idea is not to have your identity revealed, but throughout the games, depending on how players move, they may get clues as to who you are. Getting revealed is dangerous, as people will be able to attack you, but even if you're defeated, if your team wins, you win.

On september 1st 2012, Yasutaka Ikeda, designer of Shadow Hunters accused Ayu-ya for simply copying his game. Ayu-ya agreed and the game was withdrawn two days later.
family: Video Game Theme: Project Shrine Maiden

description:
La Bataille de Leipzig 1813 - La Bataille de Wiederitzsch 1813

La Bataille de Wiederitzsch depicts an encounter during the Battle of Nations, fought in October 1813 in and around Leipzig.

At La Bataille de Wiederitzsch, the French position is anchored by Jan Dombrowski’s independent command of the 27th Division of Poles, and later supported by Delmas’ 9th Division of French and various Rhine Confederation Cavalry.  This force is greatly outnumbered by the Émigré French General Langeron’s wing of the Army of Silesia, consisting of the IX and X Corps of Russian and Prussian forces.  These two corps, with tremendous artillery support, have an overwhelming superiority in numbers over the French Imperial forces.

The battle, which saw the Coalition forces push the Poles and French out of the Wiederitzsch after three brutal hours of combat, was among the most intensive of any of the battles in the Leipzig cycle.  Historically, the twin villages change hands several times.  Marshal Enterprises has developed special rules to reflect the gruesome nature of the conflict, and the almost mystical determination of the Poles to prevail over their hated Russian neighbors.

Each 20 minute turn starts with a Command Phase for both sides followed by the French Player sequence and then by the Prussian Player sequence. The sequence includes movement, cavalry charges, fire and melee. During each, morale plays a decisive role. This is a great one player on one player game but will also accommodate two players on a side also.

The original rules have been updated but not expanded to any extent. The original designers of the La Bataille Series believe the Premier Rules facilitate / expedite the most accurate and competitive play. The new Premier Rules are an update of the original rules but make it easier to find specific topics and includes some user requested rules such as command control.

New rules for the players to consider are the effects of Elite Units, Landwehr skirmishing, Russian Dragoon’s abilities, improved towns, dusk combat, the loyalties of certain units, command structures and plundering. Besides the historical battle, four scenarios are included. These provide for a cavalry on cavalry only battle, inspired attacks by von Bluecher and the ability to jump into combat for the struggle of Moeckern itself. This scenario will end whenever a player reaches a threshold casualties and disintegration of order.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Series: La Bataille (Marshal Enterprises / Clash of Arms Games)

description:
It is the 1800’s and the promise of a new future lies in the Pacific Northwest. In between lies hardships, harsh terrain and the unknown. Gathering up family and friends, you load up your wagon and head west, hoping to beat the winter weather to your new home.
Wagons West is a game of time and resource management. Each player starts with $200 which they use to purchase a wagon and supplies for the journey west. Once loaded, players start down the trail, following others or pushing ahead in an effort to save time. But the road is not without its dangers and unexpected turns. Cholera, rough terrain, accidents, shortage of food and water and treacherous river crossings all threaten to slow the party down or stop it all together.
Manage your time and resources well and with a little luck you will make it to your new home in the west.

Soviet historians tended to see the Great October Socialist Revolution as irrefutable proof of the inevitable scientific laws of economics as pronounced by Marx and interpreted by Lenin. Liberal historians tend to focus on the political and ideological background of the revolution and ignore the social and economic issues. Left wing historians, particularly Anarchist writers, tend to view the revolution as the expression of the mass will to reject authoritarian intervention in their liberty and freedom. Revisionist historians consider the revolution as arising primarily from below, that is, from the peasants and the workers. And most historians tend to take an obligatory swipe at the Tsar, Tsarina and Rasputin, and then ignore the bureaucracy, the armed forces high command (Stavka) and the bourgeois reactionaries forever after.

Rather than attempt to justify one or the other of these dogmas, perhaps it would be easier to realize that Russia in 1917 was an enormous paradox. She was one of the largest countries in the world in terms of both area and population, yet her economy was primitive and underdeveloped, and 85% of her population consisted of illiterate and superstitious peasants with no interests in anything much beyond the limits of their own villages, least of all politics. Russia was about a century behind the development of the other Great Powers, and although she was finally trying to catch up, she was handicapped by a political system and an absolute monarch that were simply unequal to the 20th Century. When the revolution finally came, it was solely Russian, which is to say, a national phenomenon with a traditional outlook; and it adhered to the Standard Operating Procedures for revolutions, which is to say, that it was initiated by the moderates and then ultimately taken over by the extremists. But to Lenin and his Bolshevik organization, the revolution was going to have a global outlook with the intent of imposing Lenin’s vision of socialism on the entire world.

Regardless of the name given to the events in Petrograd in 1917, the facts are that there was a popular revolution followed by a Bolshevik coup d’état. Petrograd 1917 starts with the popular revolution that had been initiated by the moderates, was taken over by the extremists, and then succumbed to the inevitable coup d’état. Players will find that staging a successful coup will not be as easy as it was for Lenin, who was blessed with the unwitting but invaluable assistance of Kerensky.

This is a card-based simulation for 2 to 6 players, each of whom will try to create a political organization and help it to gain influence, so that it becomes the predominant political party in Russia, either by votes or by force. The game board represents Petrograd, the capital of Russia, which was the site of most of what happened at the time. Markers and counters on the board indicate where army units, factories, bridges, railroad stations and other important buildings are located.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

boardgame: Major League Baseball by Yogi Berra
description: Players select the type of pitch, manage batting strategy, coach base running.  A die roll determines the outcome of the at bat.   Odds of a hit are based on selecting the correct batting strategy that matches the opponent's selected pitch.
publishers: Research Games, Inc. / Athol / RGI / ARC

description:
Twenty blocks move in a board of forty houses in order to block the movement of the other twenty of different color.
Initially each house is filled by one block; these are aggregated forming larger pieces that when moved to empty houses are divided into two smaller ones. 
The same movement rules result in different paths when applied to the various sizes of pieces.
The blockade can be achieved by arresting opponent's pieces, obstruction of pathways, by isolation of single blocks and protection of terminal houses. All these actions can be fought by releasing pieces, clearing pathways, by rescuing isolated blocks and attacking blocks that impede movement termination. 
A more strategic complexity is achieved with the four-player mode in teams of two; playing with half the blocks of his team each player has two opponents and one ally.

boardgame: Casemate & Cannon: Fleet Action Rules for the Age of Steam 1820-1890
description:
Casemate & Cannon brings the Age of Steam to the fingertips of the Armchair Admiral. The rules were designed for maneuver and engaging large fleets and squadrons into Close Action. A complete and comprehensive Fleet system designed for detail and fast play.

- allows for a 20 ship game to be completed in 2-3 hours
- can be used with any scale and no rebasing needed
- all major and minor nations Army lists
- allows for naval warfare all over the world
- features HRG's Naval Terrain Generation system
- complete navale lists and ship capabilities
- admiral and player aid tools
- includes complete campaign integration for Eagle's Prey
- can be used from 1820-1900
- integrates with HRG's Eagle's Prey 19th century rules

(from the back of the book)

boardgame: Mighty Morphin Power Rangers: Lord Zedd's Last Stand Game
description:
Power Rangers, the evil Lord Zedd has taken control of your Dinozords. In order to stop Lord Zedd from destroying Angel Grove and the world, the rangers must defeat Lord Zedd!

Lord Zedd has sent all of his Evil Space Aliens and Putty Patrol Warriors to stop you. Do you have what it takes to defeat them all in order to take back your Zords from the evil Lord Zedd?

Game Objective: Players need to have their Power Ranger reach the Lord Zedd Battle Space. The first player to have their Power Ranger successfully defeat the evil Lord Zedd is the winner!

Game Play: Players choose their favorite Power Ranger and decide player rank by playing rock, paper and scissors. Game movement involves rolling the die and moving your Power Ranger the number indicated along the board.

Players will battle over 20 various Evil Space Aliens from the Power Ranger series (season 1 & 2) and the Putty Patrol Warriors, as well as answering Finister Trivia questions along there way to battling the evil Lord Zedd.

boardgame: Mia and me: Der magische Trumptus
description:
A children's game set in the universe of the 2011 live action/CGI animated television series "Mia and me".

Background story: The elves are trying to find the pieces of a magical instrument, the "magical Trumptus", which are scattered all over Centopia. The Trumptus is their only hope of chasing away evil Panthea and her Munculus warriors. But where to look for the pieces? They are all over Centopia, and behind every corner there might be an evil Munculus warrior. Cautiously, the players search for the pieces. Will they be able to find all the pieces before Panthea manages to mobilise her troops?

Gameplay: The Trumptus and Munculus chits are randomised and placed into the slits in the game board. Each player gets an elf board. The Panthea board is placed in the middle of the table, next to the game board. Players take turns rolling the die. If a number from 1-3 is rolled, the corresponding number of chits is taken from the game board and placed in front of the active player's elf board. Then he/she can decide if they want to roll again or end their turn. They may repeat this step as often as they want, as long as they don't draw a Munculus chit from the board or roll the Munculus symbol.
If a player decides to end their turn, they place all the Trumptus chits of their own colour on their elf board. They return the Trumptus chits of other players' colours to the game board (putting them into slits of their choice). Then it's the next player's turn.
If a Munculus chit is drawn, the player's turn is over immediately. The Munculus chit is placed on the Panthea board. Previously drawn Trumptus chits are returned to the board and the die is handed over to the next player. If the Munculus symbol is rolled, the player's turn is over too. All previously drawn Trumptus chits are given to the players they belong to, and they place them on their elf boards.

The game ends when one player has placed all 8 Trumptus chits of their colour on their elf board. That player wins the game. The game also ends when the Panthea board has been completely filled with Munculus chits. In this case, Panthea wins. Second place goes to the player who has collected the most Trumptus chits.

The game about Good Driving, made in East Germany in 1960. The box image shows various vehicles but the car was fiction. The Trabant owning citizen could only aspire for such a car, a wish he knew would never be fulfilled.

The board shows a road network with dials at both ends. Each player starts in a different corner and the objective is to get back to the same corner first after having visited the petrol station. Cars move by the throw of a die. The rules of the road must be respected. When you move you may move left or right but you must keep on the right side of the road.

When you approach a crossroads you must turn the appropriate dial one 'space' to the left or right. The new sign or traffic light now in front of you must be obeyed.

boardgame: Der Kleine Prinz: Planet der Zeit
description:
A "The Little Prince" themed game that helps children learn the clock.

Background story: On the Planet of Time the clocks are acting up. This is the evil snake's fault, because she has coaxed the time watchman into turning back the time. Who is going to help the Little Prince and his friends to scare away the snake and bring the hours of the day and night into the correct sequence?

Setup: The starting tile is placed in the middle of the table. The time rays are placed to its sides so that a long time ray from 0 to 24 hours is created (the time rays have numbers from 0-24 printed on them). The hour tiles (showing clocks) are shuffled together with the snake tiles and put face down on the table. Each player gets a character tile and places it in front of him/herself on the table.

Aim of the game: To put as many hour tiles as possible in the correct position on the time ray and by doing so collect as many cogwheel chits as possible. Remembering where the evil snakes are hidden will help you to defeat them and win the game!

Gameplay: The start player puts his/her character tile in a position of the time ray that isn't yet taken up by an hour tile. Then he/she turns over one of the face-down tiles from the middle of the table. If it shows the same time as the position the player has put his/her character tile in (e.g. the character tile is on the "11" position of the time ray and the tile depicts a clock showing 11 o'clock), the hour tile is put in that position, the character tile is returned to the player, and he/she earns a cogwheel chit. If the hour tile doesn't match, the hour tile is returned to its old place in the middle of the table.

If a snake tile is turned over, the player has two options: First, he/she can simply return the snake tile face down to its old place. Second, he/she can return it face up and try to turn over the other two snake tiles. If he/she is successful, he/she immediately wins the game. If he/she isn't, he/she loses a cogwheel chit and then it's the next player's turn.

There are two ways the game can end:
a) The evil snake has been defeated (i.e. a player has turned over all three snake tiles). In this case there are two winners: The player who has turned over the snake tiles and the player who at this moment owns the most cogwheel chits.
b) All the hour tiles have been correctly placed on the time ray. The player who has collected the most cogwheel chits wins the game.

80 cards with 40 musical instruments and one Audio CD. 
The games of Sonix series are specifically designed to promote the following skills in your child: perception, discernment, acoustic memory, logic, observation and language skills.
